https://lambethcouncil.bookings.flow.onl/location/streatham-ice-and-lei…
The costs to skate are £14.95 which includes skate hire.
If you require a locker you will need a £1 coin (returnable).
Streatham Ice & Leisure Centre features an Olympic size ice rink (60m x 30m). The rink has plenty of room without people bumping into each other whilst ice skating.
Is this your first ODL event?
Attending your first event with OutdoorLads might feel daunting. What will happen, who will be there, and what can you expect? We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.

Title: Manchester Pride 2026 - Peel parkrun
Summary:
Join us for a Pride parkrun as we celebrate and promote
OutdoorLads
at one of the UK’s biggest and best Pride events!
🏳️‍🌈
For those of you who enjoy your weekly parkrun fix, we'll be making the most of Manchester Pride 2026 weekend and heading to Peel parkrun in Salford. If you've not heard of it, parkrun is a free, weekly, timed 5k event that takes place every Saturday at locations across the world.
We will meet at 8.45am by the parkrun sign for a group photo. I'll be wearing a red parkrun t-shirt, but feel free to wear any OutdoorLads merchandise or Pride accessories. You can register for parkrun and get your own barcode from
www.parkrun.org.uk
to scan at the end of your run to record your finish time if you wish. However, you do not need to do this to take part in the run if you don't want to.
Course description:
The course is 5k long, run on a mixture of tarmac paths and grass. This is a two lap course by the river and around a football pitch and the park. It is mainly on good quality paths except for a short section on grass, which can get muddy. The start and finish by the statue in the middle of Peel Park, near the playground.
On occasion parkruns can be cancelled due to the weather or lack of volunteers. Should this happen I will try to contact members as soon as I can.
OTHER PRIDE WEEKEND ACTIVITIES:

Title: Manchester Pride 2026 - Grindr: The Opera!
Summary:
Join us as we celebrate and promote
OutdoorLads
at one of the UK’s biggest and best Pride events!
🏳️‍🌈
Grindr: The Opera! puts the most notorious gay hook-up app into the exaggerated world of opera. This musical parody takes you on a hilarious and thought-provoking journey through the world of gay dating, focusing on the greatest catalyst for the shift: GRINDR.
With catchy songs, witty dialogue, and colourful characters, this unique and ground-breaking production explores the impact of technology on human connection and the search for meaningful relationships.
Through the experiences of four gay men who use Grindr to connect with each other,
Grindr: The Opera!
offers a satirical commentary on modern gay culture.
Devon the romantic, Tom the cynic, Jack the twink and Don the daddy meet via the medium of the Grindr app, each seeking a different type of connection.
You’ll laugh, you’ll cry, and you’ll be challenged to think deeply about the ways in which technology is shaping our lives and relationships.
But above all,
Grindr: The Opera!
will make you feel alive. With its infectious energy, dynamic performances, and unforgettable characters, this production will leave you feeling inspired, entertained, and maybe even a little bit hopeful about the future of human connection.
Further information can be found on
this website
.
Book your own ticket!
Tickets are £35 and are available
here
(or via the above website) and need to be booked by each member in advance. There is a discount code for £5 off - message the leader for this. Tickets don't seem to be allocated, so we can arrange to sit together (or in small groups depending on the space) on the night.
The ticket website shows the whole of the Manchester 'run', so make sure you book for the 7.30pm performance on Friday 28 August. Of course you can head along at any other time it's running either in Manchester or around the country (the discount code works for other performances and other locations I believe).

Title: Mid-Week Evening Walk in East Yorkshire
Summary:
This is a relatively short circular walk, 6km (3.75 miles) taking around 1 and a half hours. We start and finish in the town of Pocklington. We will walk from the town up through Pocklington Woods. We will then cross the golf course and head towards the Madhyamaka Kadampa Medidation Centre, located in an idyllic setting overlooking two small lakes. We'll then descend down to the largest lake and continue back to Pocklington through Spring Wood.
This should bring us back to the town in less than 2 hours. There is a bar with an outside terrace at the golf course with nice views we can stop at for a quick drink.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Image credits: all images provided by event Leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: This is a short walk (6km, 3.75m), maximum elevation climb 80m, along a mixture of hard standing tracks, paved roads, and grass/woodland trails (which can be very muddy).

Title: Summer Walk in East Yorkshire
Summary:
Join Lee for a 17 km/10.6 miles walk through the rolling hills around Wharram Percy and Thixendale in the North York Moors! The route will be around 440m of total ascent taking in the deserted medieval village of
Wharram Percy
, "one of the largest and best preserved of Britain's 3,000 or so known deserted medieval villages", Thixendale village and the surrounding rolling hills.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.

Image credits: all images provided by event Leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: 17Km, 440m of ascent

Title: Manchester Pride 2026 - The Vigil
Summary:
Join
OutdoorLads
as we come together for one of the most moving and meaningful moments of the Manchester Pride weekend — the
Candlelit Vigil
in Sackville Gardens.
We’ll be meeting at
The Molly House from 7:30pm
, giving us time to socialise and catch-up before walking together to
Sackville Gardens by 8:30pm
for the start of the vigil.
This event is
open to all
, and you do
not
need a Manchester Pride wristband to attend.
🕯️ About the Candlelit Vigil
Hosted in partnership with George House Trust
, which has supported people living with HIV since 1985, the vigil is a powerful evening of remembrance, reflection and unity.
As the sun sets and Sackville Gardens is illuminated by candlelight, we stand together in silence to remember those we have lost to HIV and other causes, and to show support for those who continue to suffer or face persecution simply for being who they are.
This is a space for solidarity, hope and healing – a moment to reaffirm our commitment to inclusion, visibility and compassion for our LGBTQ+ siblings across the world.
Candles will be available in the gardens for a small donation to George House Trust
, who help organise and host this incredibly special event.
Whether this is your first Manchester Pride or you’ve attended many times before, the vigil offers a moment of calm and connection to close the weekend with purpose.
We hope you can join us.

Title: Manchester Pride 2026 - The Parade!
Summary:
Join us as we celebrate and promote
OutdoorLads
at one of the UK’s biggest and best Pride events!
🏳️‍🌈
We’re back with a bang this year! To celebrate 20 incredible years of OutdoorLads, we’re levelling up - we’ll have both a
float and a group of walkers
following behind as we march through the city. Expect the same high energy, bold spirit, and pride as we mark two decades of community, adventure, and friendship.
💚 The Theme:
No Place Like Home
This year, Manchester Pride celebrates the theme "No Place Like Home." For many of us, our community is the home we found for ourselves - a place of belonging, safety, and authentic connection.
OutdoorLads has been that home for thousands of people over the last 20 years, providing a space where we can be exactly who we are. Join us - whether on the float or as part of our walking contingent—as we take to the streets of Manchester to celebrate our history, our chosen family, and the unique spirit that makes our community feel like home.
📍 The Plan
We'll gather at the meeting point (Location & Time TBC) please keep an eye on your messages and this event page as this is how we'll provide updates closer to the day. We’ll be easy to spot, just look for the red OutdoorLads T-shirts (which you’ll need to wear!).
As always, we want to showcase everything that makes OutdoorLads awesome, so if you're able, get involved by bringing gear that reflects your favourite activities:
Climbers
– Wear your harness. Bonus points for clipping on nuts, hexes or cams for a bit of jingly flair!
Cyclists / Mountain Bikers
– Ride along-side on your bike. Bells and horns encouraged for extra fun!
Walkers / Hikers
– Walking poles, rucksacks, hydration bladders (contents at your discretion!) and massive OS maps are all welcome. Bonus points if you act lost and ask the crowd for directions.
We’ll also have
flags to wave
,
stickers to hand out
, and heaps of enthusiasm to share!
Most importantly, don’t forget your red OutdoorLads T-shirt!
🎟️ Parade Wristbands
To take part in the parade, you
must
collect a wristband from our muster point.
No wristband = no parade entry.
Please arrive on time, as late arrivals last year struggled to get one.
🚫
Please note:
Dogs are not allowed in the parade. This is a restriction set by the parade organisers.
🌈 After the Parade
We’ll be spending time in the Village after the parade, both to socialise and to represent OutdoorLads to potential new members.
We'll likely break into small groups to chat to the public, hand out stickers and encourage sign-ups (ideally while enjoying a few drinks in the sunshine!).
📍 Details of where to meet after the parade will be announced on the day.
Important:
You’ll need a separate
Manchester Village Pride
ticket
to access the Village after the parade. Tickets for the Gay Village Party purchased
here
.
The
parade wristband does not
grant access to the Pride event, so we recommend getting your event wristbands in advance if you plan to join us in the Village afterwards.
More details will be added here and emailed to participants as plans develop. It’s going to be another fantastic year.
We can’t wait to see you there!
🎉

Title: Manchester Pride 2026 - Picnic in the Park
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for a picnic in the park to celebrate Manchester Pride!
We’re back!
After the fantastic success of our
2025 Picnic in the Park
, we’re thrilled to bring this event back for
Manchester Pride 2026
!
This is a fantastic opportunity to
meet new faces
,
catch up with old friends
, and ease into the celebrations before heading into the Village for the evening.
🧺 What to Bring
Your favourite picnic snacks and drinks (alcohol is allowed as the event is outside the Village cordon)
A blanket to sit on
Pride flags, face glitter, or anything else to get into the spirit!
OutdoorLads red T-shirt if you want to stand out in the crowd
No schedule or pressure – just turn up when you can and stay for as long as you like.
📍 Location
Vimto Gardens
is a peaceful green space just a few minutes' walk from both
Sackville Gardens
and the
Gay Village
. It's an ideal spot to gather before the festivities kick off.
We’ll be easy to find with our red shirts, pride flags, and picnic gear. Just look for the OutdoorLads gathering in the park!
This is a wonderful, low-key way to start your weekend, whether you’re arriving in Manchester on the Friday or just looking to enjoy some downtime with fellow OutdoorLads members before heading into the Village. No wristband required to join us for the picnic, so it’s completely open to anyone who wants to join!
We can’t wait to see you there and make more memories together at this returning favourite event!

Summary:

WE

Title: A Summer Social in Bolton - 2026
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for a summer's
Saturday
evening stroll in Bolton for our vibrant
summertime social !
We will be celebrating summer by visiting
some of the finest brew houses
, including
Ye Olde Man and Scythe
, which is thought to have been built prior to 1251 and is well renowned for its hauntings and spooky goings-on. Ye Olde Man and Scythe is full of character and has a lovely atmosphere and some fantastic regular live bands !
We will then explore the area in more detail and enjoy the fun and relaxing atmosphere. There will be plenty of opportunity for a nice ale, some nibbles, and the chance to throw some shapes on the dancefloor for those who wish to stay on a bit later.
Socials are great events for both new and existing members. If you are in any way uncertain about joining OutdoorLads, are concerned about fitness/ability, or whether OutdoorLads is for you, then please come along. Give us a go, have a drink with us and see why members have made friends through OutdoorLads. It is also a great opportunity to chat about upcoming events. These events are always friendly and enjoyed by all.
If you are interested in joining us for a meal before the event, (around 5pm ish), or if you have any other questions, please send me a message for details
.
Smart/casual dress code. Please no shorts or trackies !
Transport:
For easy access to/from Manchester and surrounding areas, the train and bus station/interchange is located in the town centre and is only a few minutes walk to the meeting point.
For more info, click
here...(https://tfgm.com/)

Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Experienced
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
"EXPERIENCED" in this instance is those that have previously climbed & can answer YES to all three of these questions:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly & confidently?
Please ensure you only sign up for this event if you
satisfy all three of the prerequisites as an experienced (independent) climber!
Never climbed before? Need a refresher on the basics? Answered NO to any of the three questions above? You will need to
attend one of the beginners (supervised) events
instead, where we will happily sign you in to be supervised (two people per leader maximum), show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely! Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week.
If you are climbing with us for the first time, whether Experienced or Beginner, it is essential that you send a note to the Event Leader telling them about your previous climbing experience & your goals!
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event as 'no-shows' are not only rude, but also frustrating for the leader & other attendees who may have travelled over an hour away to climb with you!
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing or would like a refresher, then this is just for you!! If you’ve climbed before, can safely tie into a harness & can competently belay, then there’s plenty of choices of top-rope & lead climbing on offer here.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.
You MUST register via this event page before coming to the centre!
To climb, you will need to:
Complete a
Registration Form
(please arrive early to do this in-person the first time) & pay a Registration Fee:
Annual Registration
is
£5
or
Day Registration
is
£3
Pay an
Entry Fee
directly to the climbing centre:
Standard Entry
is
£12.50
&
Bouldering Only
is
£8.50.
Hire
climbing shoes (
£3.50
) & a climbing harness (
£3.50
) if you don't own your own.
Please see Redpoint's
website
for current prices & note that prices are subject to change.
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
PLEASE NOTE:
All those attending the event must also be aware of the climbing centre's own terms & conditions of participation & abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff.
Photos:
taken & used with permission of
R. Casabuena
&
K. Jones
.
Difficulty & Distance: This event is for EXPERIENCED (independent) climbers only!!!

Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing (Basford) – Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals.
Nottingham Climbing Centre - Basford
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Nottingham Climbing Centre - Basford
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Bamford Edge Circular - DVH Way Section 1
Summary:
The Derwent Valley Trust
was set up in 1996 to promote the history, arts, wildlife and leisure opportunities that surround the river Derwent along its 55 mile course through Derbyshire. The DVH Way takes us from Ladybower Reservoir in the Peak District National Park to its end at Derwent Mouth where it flows into the River Trent near Shardlow. The Duke of Devonshire opened the walk at Chatsworth House in 2003.
The route takes us through the Chatsworth estate, Derbyshire dales, towns, villages, canals and many points of interests through the
Derwent Valley Mills World Heritage Site
. There are plenty of pubs and tea shops for refreshments along the way. The route follows the riverside path through Derby City and continues southeastwards to the historic inland port of Shardlow. Journey’s end is at Derwent Mouth where the Derwent flows into the River Trent.
The route is divided into eight sections of between  ~13 km (8 miles) and ~17 km (10.5 miles), each with a clear public transport solution. All, except the first section, are linear routes. We park at the end, travel to the start, and walk back to our cars. Sections 5, 6, 7 are brilliantly served by the East Midlands Railway Derwent Valley Line
(Derby–Matlock, hourly), which runs almost parallel to the trail the whole way. For sections (2, 3, 4 and 8), we use local buses.
Each section is a walk in itself. Join one or more, as you wish, or walk all eight sections to complete the whole route through Derbyshire. Please ask other attendees for a lift if you need one.
Section 1 — Bamford Edge Circular ~17 km (10.5 miles), total ascent 389 m (1276 ft)
The first section of the DVH Way is a scenic, wild and challenging circular route. From Hathersage we climb northwards out of the village to Bole Hill then traverse Bamford Edge - with spectacular views of Ladybower below - to reach Hatherdene, the official start point of the DVH Way. The route crosses Ladybower Dam, providing fine views of Ladybower Tor.
We continue southwards alongside the reservoir on the old Thornhill Trail - a dismantled railway - between the heights of Win Hill and Bamford Edge. The route then takes us to the riverbank for a stroll through meadows to Leadmill Bridge.
At Hathersage, the churchyard is said to be the final resting place of Robin Hood’s comrade, Little John. Charlotte Bronté stayed at the nearby vicarage in 1845. There are numerous places for refreshments and shops to browse in the village centre at the end of the walk.
The ascent is all in the first third of the route then more-or-less downhill the rest of the way. The terrain includes moorland paths at Bamford Edge then easy walking, mainly on a disused railway track, riverside paths, through woods and fields.
There is a link to the
OS Maps
route for members, below.
Well behaved
dogs are welcome
but must kept under control at all times.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.
All photos obtained online with permission to modify use and share:
Main image: geograph.org.uk/John H Darch. Image 2: flickr.com/simon gray and commons.wikimedia.org. Image 3: geograph.org.uk/Mick Garratt. Image 4: ODL.com/Mark Butterworth

Title: Touring the Birthplace of British Tourism
Summary:
Ross on Wye promotes itself as the birth place of British tourism, so why not join me as we enjoy a walk starting from this market town? We start by following the Wye away from Ross before working our way round multiple strawberry fields, but we probably won't have time to stop and pick your own!
We'll find a nice spot for lunch before preparing ourselves for the climb up Chase Hill, where you may get a view of the MOD rifle range, before we head back into Ross.
Dogs will be welcome on the walk but there are a number of stiles that larger dogs may find difficult. We also pass through a couple of farms where they would need to be kept under control.

Title: Bromley Cross to Blackrod -GM14
Summary:
This is stage 14 of 20 on the Greater Manchester Ringway, a 200 mile (322km) walking trail exploring all 10 boroughs of Greater Manchester. You can find out more about it
here
.
Highlights include:
Lovely riverside walking along the valley of Eagley Brook
The picturesque village of Barrow Bridge with its cottages and ‘Miner’s Steps’
The ascent of Winter Hill through the Smithills Estate, including a plaque commemorating the mass trespass of 1896
Panoramic views on both the ascent of Winter Hill and the descent via the mini-summit of ‘Two Lads’
The semi-ancient woodland of Wilderswood, followed by the chance to visit the little-known Tiger’s Clough waterfall.
Note that parts of the trail may be damp, muddy and slippery at any time of year and especially after periods of wet weather.
This is a linear walk
. See the options in the location details below.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.

Title: Bouldering NORTH WALES - Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads For An Evening of INDOOR BOULDERING in NORTH WALES!
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Bouldering is a form of climbing that focuses on shorter "problems" emphasising technique, balance, body positioning, and sometimes power, rather than long, high routes. Without the need for ropes, harnesses, and belaying, bouldering is an accessible activity for newcomers and is often considered the "purest" form of climbing.
Bouldering offers a wide range of benefits, including:
Physical:
Improved strength, tone, balance, power, and coordination.
Mental:
Enhanced problem-solving, focus, concentration, and body awareness.
Emotional:
Relaxation, a sense of achievement, and a feeling of mind-body connection.
Bouldering is also a fantastic way to meet new people and build lasting friendships, as it's an inherently social sport where climbers share tips and strategies to conquer the routes.
New to Bouldering?
If you're new to bouldering, it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader to learn the basics of safe bouldering. The leader can then introduce you to the rest of the group where you can refine your newly learnt skills in a social environment.
Experienced Boulderer?
If you’re new to climbing with ODL, the leader will help to introduce you to the rest of the group so you can explore the centre together.
You are responsible for your own safety and actions while bouldering. Please use your own judgement when accepting advice from OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers, regardless of your bouldering experience.
Ready to boulder?
You will need to sign up on the ODL event page. We would ask you to do this each time so that the leader has access to important information
If you are not already registered with The Boardroom Climbing centre you will need to do so when you arrive. Maybe arrive a little earlier to do so.
When you arrive at the centre you will need to pay for entry and hire climbing shoes if you don't own a pair.
Get bouldering! You’ll find most of the group already bouldering so feel free to join them. If you’re new to bouldering with ODL, the leader will be looking out for you so if you spot them, please go speak with them first.
Any questions?
If you have any questions, please contact the leader directly.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by Mark Mckee
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: Bristol Indoor Bouldering - all abilities welcome!
Summary:
The Climbing Academy is one of the best bouldering centres in the South West. With over 200 problems in a 750 square metre area there is enough to keep even the avid of boulderers occupied.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' as they are referred to tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport. For this reason, it is often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
To come along, you will need to register via the event page, and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the centre when you arrive. For entry fee prices go to:
https://www.theclimbingacademy.com/prices/
. As we start at 8pm you will pay the off-peak price.
For your first time climbing with us, especially if you are a novice climber, please contact the event leader in advance to let him know what your skills and experience are.
It is important that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
Climbing and Bouldering Participation statement
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.

information including an FAQ

Title: Kensington: Empire, Exhibitions & Culture
Summary:
Kensington is one of the most affluent & prestigious areas of London, famous for its royal heritage, beautiful urban parks & gardens, and its world-class cultural quarter of arts, museum & educational venues.
This linear leisure walk from the bustle of Kensington High Street through London's embassy quarter to Kensington Palace & Gardens and on via a loop of Hyde Park to the cultural quarter of South Kensington explores this district, and along the way seeks to explain how it developed.
For a map of the route, see the location section.
Among the sights we'll see are:
St Mary Abbot's Church
: Rebuilt by Sir George Gilbert Scott in 1872, it has the highest church spire in London at 278 feet.
Kensington Palace Gardens
: A private road under the control of the Crown Estate, it comprises many fine mansions built in the mid-19th century that now house foreign embassies.
Kensington Palace
: Originally built in 1605, but completely reconstructed in the late-17th century for William & Mary, this is still a private residence for the Royal Family, although some of the state rooms are open to the public. The white marble statue of Queen Victoria outside was sculpted by her daughter Princess Louise in 1893. Next to the palace is the Sunken Garden with its statue of Diana, Princess of Wales unveiled by her sons in 2021.
Kensington Gardens
: Covering 274 acres, this area was once the private gardens of Kensington Palace before opening to the public in 1841. Among the places we'll visit will be the Physical Energy & Peter Pan statues, the Italian Gardens, and The Arch sculpture by Henry Moore.
Hyde Park
: Originally a private hunting ground for Henry VIII, it was open to the public in 1637 and separated from Kensington Gardens in 1728. It covers 351 acres, so with Kensington Gardens forms a 'green lung' for London of almost one square mile. Within it, the Serpentine Lake was formed in 1730 by damming the Westbourne River. The Crystal Palace, housing the Great Exhibition of 1851, was sited between the Serpentine & South Carriage Drive.
Albert Memorial
: This memorial to Prince Albert, consort to Queen Victoria, was commissioned by the grieving queen after his death in 1861. Designed by Sir George Gilbert Scott, it was completed by 1875. The spire is 176 feet high, and the monument is surrounded by sculptures representing the arts & sciences, and the four major continents.
Royal Albert Hall
: Facing the Albert Memorial, the Royal Albert Hall was opened in 1871 by Queen Victoria and was built with the help of profits from the 1851 Great Exhibition. It has a seating capacity of nearly 5,300 and hosts nearly 400 events a year, including the BBC Proms concerts.
Queen's Tower
: This 287-foot tall tower stands in the middle of the campus of Imperial College, London, and was formerly the central tower of the Imperial Institute, a 700-foot long building that existed from 1893 to 1957. It was saved from demolition following a campaign by the future Poet Laureate Sir John Betjeman.
Brompton Oratory
: Also known as the London Oratory, this is one of the finest Catholic churches in London, constructed
between 1869 & 1884 in the Italian Renaissance style.
The Thin House
: Completed in 1887 on land in Thurloe Square left over from the construction of South Kensington station, this bizarre building is 6 feet wide at its narrowest point. Originally artists' studios, the block was later converted to flats.
Dogs
Dogs are welcome on this walk, but please be aware that they are required to be on a lead in certain areas of the Royal Parks and when in proximity to wildlife (such as the ducks next to the various lakes). They will also need to be on a lead adjacent to traffic. They cannot be taken into Brompton Oratory.
Is this your first ODL event?
Attending your first event with OutdoorLads might feel daunting. What will happen, who will be there, and what can you expect? We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.

Photos

Title: BIG20: BIG Wye Weekender (Hostel)
Summary:
The wait is finally over and we're ready to tell you all about our new BIG Event ... and it's a BIG20 event where we will be celebrating fabulous 20 years of OutdoorLads!
Nestled along one of the UK’s most scenic rivers, the Wye Valley is a haven for outdoor enthusiasts seeking both adventure and tranquillity. With its dramatic limestone cliffs, ancient woodlands, and winding river views, the area offers a breath-taking backdrop for everything from exhilarating climbs to peaceful walks. Whether you’re hiking along panoramic ridge trails, cycling quiet country lanes, or gliding through the valley by kayak, the Wye Valley delivers an unforgettable connection with nature.
Our location for the weekend is just as picturesque, a Georgian Farmhouse perched on the top a hill overlooking the surrounding valleys, which has been converted into a Outdoor Education centre and recently brought back into community ownership, the site is all ours for the Bank Holiday Weekend.
⛺ Choose Your Base Camp
We have a range of options to keep everyone happy. You need to book on either the Hostel or Camping event page and then add on your Car Parking, Teepee and Food Options to make the weekend your own.
Hostel
Camping
Teepee Camping Add-on
🥾 Getting Out More
The Wye Valley has options for everyone to Get Out More and on both Saturday and Sunday our Leaders will be putting on a range of activities which we hope will include options for cycling, climbing, water sports, cultural and heritage activities and of course a walk or two!!
There will also be some options for something a little different held on the site. We'll list the event pages below when we have them. If you're a Leader and keen to lead something, get in touch!
🚗 Getting To The Site
Parking is very limited at the site, so we've booked some additional capacity in a couple of other nearby locations. So, your parking options are...
Bronze Zone Parking
FREE
Pull up at the venue, drop your bags, drive 5 minutes down the road, and enjoy a 20-minute scenic walk back to the venue. Ideal if you don't need your car through the weekend.
Silver Zone Parking
£10
Just five minutes walk around the corner from the venue entrance.
Gold Zone Parking
£20
Park right at the venue entrance - very limited supply.
🍻 Evening Entertainment
We have an incredible lineup of evening activities to keep the energy high and the good times rolling! Here is what is waiting for you:
Woodland Fire Pit:
Just a short walk from the bar, this is the perfect spot to unwind, toast some marshmallows, and catch up with friends old and new.
The Main House:
Explore a variety of indoor spaces! Chill out in the
Quiet Lounge
, challenge friends to board games in the
Studio
, or show some ankle in the
Ballroom
.
🎧 Saturday Night Silent Disco!
To celebrate 20 years of OutdoorLads, we’re bringing you a fantastic Silent Disco experience! It’s the perfect way to dance the night away and show off your best moves without disturbing anyone heading to bed early. Depending on the weather, we’ll be partying under the stars in the marquee or lighting up the Ballroom. Get ready for a brilliant, unique night!
🍔 Fuelling the Weekend
Look out for meal booking pages in the weeks before the event. You can pre-book Friday and Saturday evening meals, breakfasts for Saturday, Sunday and Monday and packed lunches. Sunday evening meal from an outside caterer (TBC) is included.
You are of course welcome to self-cater for any part of the weekend. There is a small kitchen available for all to use, which features a small domestic cooker, fridge, microwave and kettle. You'll need to supply your own cooking and eating equipment.
BBQs are permitted, raised off the ground ensuring no damage is caused to the grass. However, this is subject to Forestry England restrictions if the weather has been particularly dry.
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs - they are not allowed on site.
Campervans, caravans, motorhomes:
Due to the venues restrictions, we are unable to accept campervans, caravans and motorhomes.
Images:
Photos taken by and used with permission from OutdoorLads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: As much or a little as you please!

Title: BIG20: BIG Wye Weekender (Camping)
Summary:
The wait is finally over and we're ready to tell you all about our new BIG Event ... and it's a BIG20 event where we will be celebrating fabulous 20 years of OutdoorLads!
Nestled along one of the UK’s most scenic rivers, the Wye Valley is a haven for outdoor enthusiasts seeking both adventure and tranquillity. With its dramatic limestone cliffs, ancient woodlands, and winding river views, the area offers a breath-taking backdrop for everything from exhilarating climbs to peaceful walks. Whether you’re hiking along panoramic ridge trails, cycling quiet country lanes, or gliding through the valley by kayak, the Wye Valley delivers an unforgettable connection with nature.
Our location for the weekend is just as picturesque, a Georgian Farmhouse perched on the top a hill overlooking the surrounding valleys, which has been converted into a Outdoor Education centre and recently brought back into community ownership, the site is all ours for the Bank Holiday Weekend.
⛺ Choose Your Base Camp
We have a range of options to keep everyone happy you need to book on either the Hostel or Camping event page and then add on your Car Parking, Teepee and Food Options to make the weekend your own.
Hostel
Camping
Teepee Camping Add-on
🥾 Getting Out More
The Wye Valley has options for everyone to Get Out More and on both Saturday and Sunday our Leaders will be putting on a range of activities which we hope will include options for cycling, climbing, water sports, cultural and heritage activities and of course a walk or two!!
There will also be some options for something a little different held on the site. We'll list the event pages below when we have them. If you're a Leader and keen to lead something, get in touch!

🍻 Evening Entertainment
We have an incredible lineup of evening activities to keep the energy high and the good times rolling! Here is what is waiting for you:
Woodland Fire Pit:
Just a short walk from the bar, this is the perfect spot to unwind, toast some marshmallows, and catch up with friends old and new.
The Main House:
Explore a variety of indoor spaces! Chill out in the

Studio

🎧 Saturday Night Silent Disco!
To celebrate 20 years of OutdoorLads, we’re bringing you a fantastic Silent Disco experience! It’s the perfect way to dance the night away and show off your best moves without disturbing anyone heading to bed early. Depending on the weather, we’ll be partying under the stars in the marquee or lighting up the Ballroom. Get ready for a brilliant, unique night!
🍔 Fuelling the Weekend
Look out for meal booking pages in the weeks before the event. You can pre-book Friday and Saturday evening meals, breakfasts for Saturday, Sunday and Monday and packed lunches. Sunday evening meal from an outside caterer (TBC) is included.
You are of course welcome to self-cater for any part of the weekend. There is a small kitchen available for all to use, which features a small domestic cooker, fridge, microwave and kettle. You'll need to supply your own cooking and eating equipment.
BBQs are permitted, raised off the ground ensuring no damage is caused to the grass. However, this is subject to Forestry England restrictions if the weather has been particularly dry.

Title: Vale of Llangollen and Dee Valley Camp 2026
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for a weekend in the beautiful Vale of Llangollen and Dee Valley, part of the Clwydian Range and Dee Valley Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty!
We will be camping in Carrog, in between the River Dee and Llangollen (heritage) Railway, a stone throw from a pub with outstanding views across the valley.  Our location provides the opportunity for varied activities, including
walking, road cycling, canoeing
across the Pontcysyllte Aqueduct (the highest aqueduct in the world) and catching a
heritage train
up and down the valley.
The main event on the Saturday will be a hike around the surrounding hills. If that is not for you, you are welcome to head off and do your own thing.  There is the heritage railway adjacent to the site, and lovely river a short walk from the campsite where you can chill out (bring a picnic blanket!) On the Sunday, there will likely be a shorter walk, or maybe a canoe or steam train ride depending on interest. Carrog station is 50m from the campsite with steam and diesel trains straight to Llangollen and Corwen.
There is a local pub that serves food.  We have a group booking for the Saturday evening, which is optional of course. We'll be in touch with attendees a few weeks beforehand to get pre-orders. There aren't any other shops or services in walking distance, so best to stock up on you way with food and drink for the weekend.
We are in the group field which will be for the
exclusive use of OutdoorLads
, though we share facilities with the main campsite.  The campsite allows BBQ'S but campfires must be raised well off the ground so as to not damage the grass.
Waitlist
: If this event is fully booked and you want to attend,
add yourself to the waitlist
, because places often become available closer to the date, and chances are you will get a place.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are allowed and remain the owners responsibility. They will need to stay on a lead on the campsite.
Caravans, Campervans, Motorhomes
: There are a handful of spaces and 10 electrical hook-ups.  Please message the leader to pre-book a pitch.
Photos
: By W Allaway and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Range of events to choose depending over the weekend

Title: Oxford Indoor Bouldering
Summary:
Hold on tight fellas (pun intended), OutdoorLads is now running a monthly indoor bouldering session in Oxford!
I'm sure many of us are no strangers to this kind of event, having become a very popular addition to ODL's catalogue with other chapters established in London, Bristol, Reading, Manchester and Sheffield.
For those new to the sport indoor bouldering is a form of rock climbing performed
without ropes or harness
on artificial walls (~3-4m) with crash mats underneath for safety. Here your technique and your problem solving ability counts just as much as your strength, so be prepared to work out both body and mind, all in a very social and friendly atmosphere (seriously, the puzzle solving nature of this sport acts as an amazing ice breaker when chatting to new people). I will also be there to welcome you into the group and answer any questions you may have, as well as offer climbing tips should you need them 😊.
We will be meeting at the
Gallery Bouldering
center located in the heart of Oxford, offering over 100m of bouldering wall (and growing) with a diverse range of routes catering for beginners all the way to Font 7C, so that there is something for all skill levels to enjoy. Afterwards we may also pop in for a pint at one of the many nearby pubs.
As with most climbing gyms there is a
waiver
that you will need to sign to check that you understand the risks involved and the proper climbing etiquette to follow to keep others and yourself safe while climbing. This can be filled out at reception but can also be done ahead of time by following the link
here
.
Please also note that this is a
liquid chalk only
gym, which can be readily purchased at reception.
Hope to see you there!
Difficulty & Distance: A range of climbs are available for all different skill levels to enjoy

Title: Eryri: Rivers, Mines, Forests, Views
Summary:
Our Route:
Join us on this linear (one-way) 8 mile cross country heritage walk through the lowlands in the heart of Eryri National Park.
We'll pass the famous Swallow falls and hike across the Gwydir Forest from Betwys Y Coed to the Conwy Valley at Llanrwst.
Lead and zinc mining once dominated area, the industry is long gone but the remains of Cyffty and Hafna mines have been partially restored and made safe for visitors.
Our route intially follows the River Llugwy out of Betws' where we will see the 'rock cannons' used for setting off fireworks for celebrations.
After an hour on the Snowdonia Slate trail long distance path we will find ourselves at the famous Swallow Falls where the river plunges east towards it's junction with the River Conwy.
From the falls we will turn north and there's a bit of ascent to do until we reach a pleasant viewpoint to stop for lunch.
We cross forest-clad hillsides, and the next part of the route takes us past some rather interesting lead mines which retain some of the structures and buildings from the time.
The last mile or so takes us back down to Llanrwst and an historical cafe by the river,  for optional tea and cakes etc.
Photos:
AndyM, event leader - taken from a previous trip, these give you a sense of the landscape you'll see.
Bad Weather:
This event will be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain, high winds, lightning or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case.
Dogs:
are welcome to join us on this event but please note the following:
We may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead. There are places with steep drops into fast moving water where an adventurous dog may be in peril.
Not everyone likes dogs so please ensure your furry pal doesn't worry other members.
There may be obstacles to carry your dog across, and your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Difficulty & Distance: 8 miles with 1400' of height gain underfoot - gravel, rocks ,tree roots, tarmac

Title: Late summer camping in the beautiful Meon Valley
Summary:
Celebrate the end of the summer camping season in the Moen Valley, near the western edge of the South Downs.  We will be on the grounds of the Bereleigh Estate, which is the home to the Tyrwhitt-Drake family.
Campsite facilities include full shower blocks, washing up areas and they are dog-friendly (£4.00 per dog per night).  Campfires are allowed so there will be 'smores!!!  The site cannot accommodate caravans, but campervans are allowed; however there are no electric hook-ups.
Friday Afternoon:
Arrive any time after 13.30, look for the ODL flag and gazebo, pitch up, and settle in.  We will be in the Paddock Camping Area, and when we you arrive, we will simply text the Reception team and they will check us in.  Dinner this evening can be around the tents or at The Dell, which is the on-site cafe, with pizzas (best ordered in advance - more on that later!) and paninis.
Saturday morning:
Should the weather cooperate, the plan is to venture out on this 8.5 mile hike to
East Moen Stone Bridge
, with a potential stop at
Ye Olde George Inn
before heading back to the campground.  Lunch will need to be brought along, or there may be some offerings at the local off-license in East Moen.
Saturday Evening
After unwinding back at the campground, dinner can again be cooked around the tents or at
The Dell
.  If we are feeling adventurous, we could head back to Ye Olde George.
Sunday
If the weather is decent, breakfast can be a bit relaxed but we need to be off site by midday.
New to Camping ?
If this is your first event, don't be put off if you lack some of the necessary equipment; just message me on
chris.keivit@outdoorlads.com
or on Telegram -
@chriskk_fafner
.
Campfires & BBQ's
: This event may include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Food and drink
: In addition to the offerings at the site, the nearest grocery stores are Aldi, Lidl, M&S, Tesco and Waitrose in Petersfield (if you are coming from the north / northeast).  If arriving from the Winchester direction, all the major stores are there, as well as a Tesco Express and Coop in New Alresford.
Dogs:
The site is dog friendly (kept on the leads), but at a cost of £4.00, payable locally.
Photos:
All photos have been provided by OutdoorLads members and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: A late summer campsite with a Saturday walk

Title: Mountain Walk Leaders Training Weekend
Summary:
Note:
This event is for existing mountain walk leaders and those who have booked their mountain walk leader assessment only. Please only sign up if you fall into one of those two categories.
The purpose of this event is for mountain walk leaders to get together for a weekend of peer-to-peer training, exchange of ideas and discuss experiences leading walks in the mountains. The event is heavily subsidised by OutdoorLads, to support leader training and development.
Over the weekend, we'll aim to get out in the local hills and practice some mountain skills. These could include:
Navigation in poor visibility
Route choice on difficult and steep terrain
Managing groups (in poor weather or with differing abilities etc.)
Assisting unconfident walkers on steep exposed terrain
The exact topics we'll cover can be decided over the weekend depending on what everyone wants to cover.
We'll be staying in the Fylde Mountaineering Hut (Little Langdale Cottage) on the Friday and Saturday night. Breakfasts and Saturday evening meal will be included, with the Friday evening meal being the usual ODL buffet.
Dogs
: This event is not suitable for dogs.
Image / photo credits
: Images belong to Nick Read or Fylde Mountaineering Club and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: We'll be walking on steep ground and off path in mountainous terrain.

Title: A Grade 3 Classic Scramble Event in Eryri
Summary:
If you like your scrambling on the challenging side this could be the event for you, with this
Eryri (Snowdonia) Classic Grade 3 Scramble Event,
which takes place over two days.
What is a Grade 3 Scramble?
A scramble of this grade is a
serious
undertaking with
big
drops and vertical sections
, and should only tackled by
experienced scramblers
who have the mindset for rock climbing. We will need to use a rope for some of the pitches of easy rock climbing (mod-Vdiff) where the exposure will be high, or where the scramble of a particular waterfall could be
hazardous
. The route could also contain steep grassy slopes which offer no hand-holds and a
slip could be fatal
. Escaping from these type routes can be problematic and only achieved by means of an abseil.
Exposure will be high
in many parts along the route.
Our scramble route selection will be made on the morning of the event at our meeting point and will mainly be dependent on the weather conditions (and aspirations of the team). The routes in-scope will be:
Dolmen Ridge - Glyder Fach Main Face
Cneifon Arete via Idwal Staircase
Bryants Gully
Atlantic Slab
All the routes are fabulous and whichever one we choose you’ll have a day remember, however these are all BIG routes and you will need to have a
high level of mountain fitness
.
You will need to be an
experienced scrambler/climber
with some rock climbing skills, i.e. tie onto a rope, belay using a belay plate and be able to remove gear
.
Along with the kit your would normally take for a day in the mountains, you will need to bring:
Harness & Helmet
Belay plate
Prussic
Nut key
Unfortunately, due to the technical nature of the day I can only take 2 people.
Please only click into this event if you have experience of at least completing grade 2 scrambles and have the rock climbing skills/equipment listed above. If you have any questions or concerns about your experience/suitablity then please drop me a message.
Please note that the event may be liable to change or cancellation due to inclement weather.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list. Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
Image
/
photo
credits
:  All images (c)
Andy
with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: Exposure, big drops, vertical climbing sections (mod-vdiff) but on good holds, loose rock, waterfall with wet slippery rocks and around 900 m of up (whichever route we do)

Title: Dartmoor Circular: Forests, Tors, and Ancient Stones
Summary:
Join us for a spectacular, varied circular day hike right through the atmospheric heart of northeastern Dartmoor. This route truly has a bit of everything: rushing rivers, deep evergreen forests, exposed ridge lines, stunning panoramic tors, and some of the finest prehistoric monuments the moor has to offer.
Setting out from near the iconic Warren House Inn, we’ll start by dropping south towards the peaceful valley of Soussons Down, winding through the trees before looping west past historic farmsteads towards Postbridge. Here, we'll cross the East Dart River near its famous ancient clapper bridge.
From Postbridge, we begin our steady northward march along the edge of the open moorland, tracking beneath Hartland Tor and Stannon Tor, with wide-open views expanding out to the west. We'll climb up towards Grey Wethers—the striking twin bronze-age stone circles nestled right on the edge of the woods.
Our route then takes a scenic, sheltered turn into the pine-scented tracks of Fernworthy Forest, skirting the southern edge of the beautiful Fernworthy Reservoir. After a quick stop to admire the water and the nearby hut circles, we’ll break back out onto the open moor via Chagford Common and Hurston Ridge. Our final stretch carries us south along the ridge line, enjoying commanding views of the landscape before returning to Warren House Inn for a well-earned drink.
Dogs
Dogs are welcome to join us on this event but we do ask the following:
Please appreciate the fact that not everyone is a dog owner or lover- especially when we stop to eat
Please ensure you adhere to the Countryside Code at all times - see (
Keeping Dogs Under Effective Control)
If your dog is uncontrolled and strays in open land frightening other animals or livestock, the leader is supported by the OutdoorLads board of trustees to ask you to leave the event as this is not acceptable behaviour.
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by Stephen Clifford and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: 14 miles in total, some of it off path across potentially boggy moorland

Title: Icknield Way: Pegsdon Hills
Summary:
Join Alex for this slightly longer 14-mile Chiltern circular walk via the Icknield Way.
We will begin in the Hertfordshire town of Hitchin and our route will take us south out of the the town centre. Here we will turn west and follow the Chiltern Way into undulating countryside and ascend the beginnings of the Chiltern escarpment as we approach the village of Great Offley, named after the Mercian King Offa. After leaving the village we will turn north, passing the the 12th-century church of Mary Magdalene, and head out into open fields for another couple of miles.
Shortly after crossing into Bedfordshire we will reach the Pegsdon Hills, with wide-ranging views to the north. We may see some Highland cattle grazing as we join the the Icknield Way and follow it the along ridge of the hills towards the trig point at Deacon Hill. Our route will descend sharply before we head into woodland, passing by the edge of the Knocking Hoe nature reserve which also has good views of the surrounding farmland and villages.
Continuing on the Icknield Way we will head east towards the pleasant village of Pirton where the parish church of St Mary sits beside the remaining earthworks of a castle. From here we will walk back towards Hitchin, entering the town via the Oughtonhead nature reserve with its herd of English longhorn cattle. We will pass through the town centre, via Market Place and another St Mary's church, before heading back towards the station.
Don't waste that space!
Summer means lots of members looking to get out more so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are welcome but please bear in mind not everyone likes dogs as much as you. Some of the fields we cross may contain livestock or horses so make sure this will not cause issues before bringing your dog along. Make sure they have the stamina for 14 miles.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse

Title: The Ancient Paths of Vernham Dean
Summary:
This circular walk begins in the peaceful village of Vernham Dean, nestled in the northern reaches of the Test Valley, where the chalk downs rise in sweeping folds.
The route sweeps through Hippenscombe, a secluded combe whose rolling grasslands reveal the limestone and chalk geology that has shaped this landscape for millions of years. Crossing into the historic Connaught Estate, walkers follow tracks once tied to aristocratic hunting grounds, now a haven for red kites, deer, and seasonal orchids. The path continues to Tidcombe, a hamlet with Saxon origins and timeless flint cottages, before turning towards Fosbury. Here, the impressive Fosbury Camp, an Iron Age hillfort perched high on the downs, offers commanding views and a glimpse into ancient settlement patterns.
The
route
Is it dog friendly?
All my walks are dog friendly, however please have your dog under control at all times. There will be livestock on route. Ensure you abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h

Title: Beam, Steam and Wind - Great Bedwyn Historical Stroll
Summary:
Our walk starts in Great Bedwyn, a small village with a grand past, once wielded political weight as a "rotten borough," sending MPs to Parliament from its modest hearths. But today, it invites us for a stroll steeped not in politics, but Wind & Water.
The first of our two historical monuments is Wilton Windmill, standing proud on the Wiltshire skyline since 1821, was born from necessity when the Kennet & Avon Canal stole the wind out of water transport. The villagers, unwilling to let trade blow away, erected this four-sailed beauty. By the mid-19th century, its mighty sails ground wheat into flour for local bakers. After years of silence, it was lovingly restored in the 1970s. Today, it’s not just a relic—flour is still milled here on special days, and visitors can see the intricate wooden machinery. It’s a monument to resilience, where wind-powered tradition meets the modern-day, and where the sails still spin tales of a community’s spirit. We will visit the grounds of the Windmill, and there will be opportunity to enter the Windmill (subject to no guided tours taking place at the same time)
We then continue our walk towards the Crofton Beam Engine, a colossal 1812 creation. This isn’t just any beam engine, it’s the only one still puffing in the world. Originally tasked with pumping water uphill to keep the Kennet & Avon Canal afloat, it’s a marvel of engineering. It once powered trade routes, lifting canal boats across chalky hills. It still steams on select weekends, a living giant that pulls us back to the canal era. Stand in its presence, and you’ll feel the pulse of industrial ambition echoing into today. (We are visiting on a non steaming day)
The
Route
Is it dog friendly?
All my walks are dog friendly, however please have your dog under control at all times. There will be livestock on route. Ensure you abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
