Title: BIG20 New Forest Woods, Heath and the Forest's oldest church
Summary:
A
short Sunday morning walk is an ideal way to finish off the
BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend
taking in some of the best areas of the National Park in the immediate vicinity of the hostel.
We will be comfortably finished before 1.30pm which should still give plenty time to travel home after the walk.
This event is for the members attending the
BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend
and will be available to book from 5pm on the 12th June
The New Forest is well known for its free roaming livestock which play an important part in sculpting the landscape, and we should see plenty of ponies (and likely some young foals) in the second half of the walk as we cross the open landscape of Setley Plain. Before that, however, we'll pass through Roydon Woods NNR (National Nature Reserve) which is particularly famous for its stunning display of bluebells in the spring, although clearly we'll be too late in the season to enjoy the full display.
Our
route
heads directly out from the hostel into Roydon Woods Nature Reserve which is an impressive patchwork of wildlife rich habitats. We'll pause by the Lymington River which is known to be the home of otters and kingfishers (I've seen plenty of one, but yet to see any of the other!), and then resume on uphill through shady hazel coppices and pine plantations of Sandy Down.
After crossing the main road we'll head on out onto the open heathland of the forest, passing by Setley Pond. The pond is a former gravel pit and is a popular location for radio controlled yacht enthusiasts. On Boxing Day, it is the location of the hotly contested Setley Cup.
Most of the remainder of the route into Brockenhurst takes us over the open heaths among the free roaming ponies, donkeys and cattle. Visitors to the forest often mistakenly refer to the "wild ponies" but all the livestock are owned by forest commoners who turn their animals out to graze the land, but remain responsible for their welfare.
Coming back into the town, we'll walk along Brockenhurst High Street with opportunities to get last minute supplies for the journey home, or the chance for those with a bit more time to stop for refreshments from the shops, pubs and restaurants in the town if desired!
Returning back to the hostel, we'll pass by the oldest church in the New Forest. St Nicholas Church was mentioned in the Domesday Book, and it is believed that many Norman and Plantagenet kings such as William II worshipped here when they came to stay at the then neighbouring Manor when visiting the Forest for hunting trips.
Brockenhurst (and specifically the very location of the hostel!) was the site of the No. 1 New Zealand General Hospital during World War I where over 20,000 NZ casualities were treated. The churchyard has 100 Commonwealth War Graves, 93 of which are New Zealanders. A large service of commemoration is held at the church each April on the Sunday closest to ANZAC Day.
A very short distance after the church, we'll be back at the hostel ready for your return home.
All images used on this page taken by the event leader and permission granted for use by ODL
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 12km (7 miles) with 120m of ascent.

Title: BIG20: New Forest Celebration - Happy as a Pig in a Brewery
Summary:
As part of the BIG20:
New Forest Celebration Weekend
- join us for a local brewery tour and tasting.
This event will be available to book from the 12th June at 5pm.
Many an ODL walk has ended with a drink or two at a local pub, so what could be more fitting on one of our BIG20 weekends than to go on a brewery tour.
Starting at Tile Barn, it's just a short walk to the brewery, where one of the brewery team will take you on not only on a journey through the history of the brewery, but a journey through the brewery itself explaining how beer gets from a sack of grain to their refreshing range of PIG BEERs. You'll be able to get hands on with some of the ingredients and of course sample plenty of their beer throughout. You'll even get one of their bar blades to take home with you at the end as a souvenir!
IMPORTANT: You will need to book directly with the venue using the following link - note places are limited. Cost is £15
https://www.pigbeer.com/event-details/brewery-tour-17
Please note - this event is for members joining us for the
BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend
Difficulty & Distance: Lowland walk to a brewery and tour round

Title: BIG20 New Forest: Camp Glee, Afternoon Tea, Vitamin Sea
Summary:
As part of the BIG20:
New Forest Celebration Weekend
- a walk to Sway and MIlford with afternoon tea.
This event will be available to book from the 12th June at 5pm and is only for attendees of the
BIG20 New Forest Celebration Weekend
.
If you join this event please note that it includes afternoon tea (which will cost you £22.50), for which places have been booked. If you do not want to have afternoon tea, you are welcome to have a cream tea or tea and cake, but places for this cannot be booked and you might have to wait. If the cost of afternoon tea is prohibitive, do consider using the OutdoorLads Foundation (see below).
Reasoning that we'll be on holiday celebrating OutdoorLads and therefore prepared to splash out, refreshments on this bespoke day walk will be afternoon tea amid the rambling roses and box hedges of a pretty gardens near the Victorian village of Milford-on-Sea. Milford is at the end of the walk and it is where we'll catch the bus to take us back to Brockenhurst. As a result, the walk distance will be nicely do-able on a warm July day and there'll be plenty of opportunities for drinks and ice creams. While heading to Milford we'll go from classic New Forest heathland to pastures and arable land, see a fantastical folly, handsome homes, mucky mudflats and a beach where we can bask in the sun.
The sights:
Sway:
Farms and smallholdings surrounding quite a suburbanised place on the edge of the heathlands and woodlands. At a slight distance we'll see Sway Tower, 66 metres tall and Grade II listed (built, it is said, with the posthumous assistance of Sir Christopher Wren, via a medium) as his mausoleum and an advertisement for Portland Cement by Andrew Thomas Turton Peterson from 1879–1885. Inspired by Mughal architecture, and at the time, the first major building in Britain to be built entirely from concrete, it remains the tallest non-reinforced concrete structure in the world. The perpetual light intended for the top was vetoed by Trinity House.
Braxton Gardens, Lymore:
Formal and cottage gardens created in 2011 that only patrons of the tea rooms can visit in the hamlet of Lymore near Milford. 12 of us are booked in to have afternoon tea. See the website
here
.
Keyhaven:
A pretty hamlet with a harbour beside Keyhaven Marshes and behind Hurst Spit. There are no shops, but some attractive cottages and a pub.
Keyhaven Marshes:
Forming in the lee of Hurst Spit, the combined area of the two nature reserves covers over 1200 hectares. It includes mudflats, salt marshes, shingle banks, coastal grazing marshes, and saline lagoons. The nature reserves support important populations of birds and rare and specialist plants and invertebrates. Salt harvesting was a source of income for local people from Roman times to 1865 when the last salt pan closed.
Milford-on-Sea:
An attractive if suburbanised coastal village overlooking the western Solent towards the Isle of Wight. Recorded in the Domesday Book, it developed from the Saxon settlement of Melleford, later shaped by agriculture, smuggling, and limited Victorian resort ambitions. Its historic core centres on the village green and High Street. Key landmarks include the Norman-origin All Saints' Church, Milford-on-Sea, with medieval additions. Today it is a residential and tourist village with beaches, Keyhaven marshes, and access to the Forest.
The
route
(please click the clink to see it at the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Heading northeast up Tilebarn Lane we'll walk through Roydon Woods along a bridleway which willn then veer west emerging from the woods to cross Widden Bottom and Milking Pound Bottom. We'll emerge from these at Coombes Lane which we'll follow along with Kings Lane and South Sway Lane. At Flexford Lane we'll head further south via a byway and a bridleway to Everton. Wainsford Road and West Lane will bring us round to the west, where we'll take Lymore Lane heading southeast to Braxton Gardens and lunch. Heading further south alongside Lymore Lane we'll reach Keyhaven and the coast. We'll then follow the Solent Way west to Hurst Spit and then northwest to Milford. After a spell at the beach we'll head northwest up Sea Rad to the centre of Milford to catch the bus back, or go to a pub and then catch the bus back.
Swimming:
This event does not include swimming as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance. If you wish to swim, you do so at your own risk. Milford-on-Sea beach currently enjoys good water quality but check
here
.
Getting the bus back:
We'll board the no. 81 bus back to Brockenhurst from the bus stop on the High Street (adj Barnes Lane) at 16.32. This will cost £3, and cash and contactless are accepted.
The OutdoorLads Foundation:
If the cost of the afternoon tea is daunting, do consider using the OutdoorLads Foundation. This enables paying members to receive up to £200 in total a year towards any expense they incur participating in an OutdoorLads event. Just apply simply and confidentially
here
, at least two weeks before the event.
(Picture credits: all pictures taken by the leader on his recce on 12 July 2026.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 17 km (10.5 miles); total ascent is 102 m; relief is gently undulating; terrain is gravel, tarmac, dirt and beach shingle.

Title: BIG20 New Forest Weekend - Beaulieu House & Motor Museum
Summary:
As part of the BIG20:
New Forest Celebration Weekend
- A visit to Beaulieu House & Motor Museum.
Located in the heart of the new forest, Beaulieu House & Motor Museum. In 1952 the Palace House and Gardens were opened to the public for the first time. For the grand opening, five Veteran cars were displayed in the entrance hall of the house by Lord Montague. From these humble beginnings the Motor Museum would develop, becoming the National Motor Museum in 1972.
We will meet at the hostel site at Tile Barn Outdoor Centre to arrange car sharing before we head off to Beaulieu - an approx. 15 minute drive away. We will likely go around as a group, there are many attractions at Beaulieu which are included in your ticket price including the National Motor Museum, Beaulieu Abbey, Beaulieu Palace House, historic gardens. Plus smaller exhibits including on screen cars and the Secret Army exhibit. Not forgetting the monorail which runs through the motor museum and gardens.
Tickets are £31.50 on the day or £28.35 if bought in
advance
.
Further information on Beaulieu
here
Images courtesy of Beaulieu.
Please note:
This event is exclusively available to members attending the
BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend
.
Difficulty & Distance: Cultural & Heritage

Title: Wanderlust in Watlington
Summary:
What is the SE500 Challenge?
The SE500 is a proposed series of challenging hill walks in the South East. They include 500 meters of ascent and a distance of around 20km (12.5 miles). Sometimes paths may be steep; other walks may include ups and downs. That’s more than the average lowland and hill walk, and great practice for a mountain! An average man can expect to burn 2000 calories on one of these walks. Why not build up your fitness and gain a sense of achievement? Along the way, see some of the South East’s most beautiful countryside, dramatic hills and natural panoramas along the way. Look out for more of these walks around the South East over the coming months.
This route definitely plays into the ascent and descent aspect of the challenge, with two thirds of the route either ascending or descending the Watlington SE500 is up there when it comes to calorie burning!
We start the walk in the charming town of Watlington which is nestled in the scenic Chiltern Hills. The town boasts a history dating back to the 6th century, with medieval streets and historic buildings creating a unique atmosphere. Watlington Hill is a popular spot for nature enthusiasts, offering stunning views and ideal walking paths. The town has a growing reputation as a creative center with a lively community of artists and musicians, hosting a range of annual events celebrating the arts, music, TV, and film, including Midsomer Murders, Inspector Morse, Poirot, Great Expectations, Sherlock Holmes, Philomena, and many more. Watlington is one of the few remaining market towns to have its own award-winning butchers, artisan chocolatiers, fresh fruit and vegetable market, florists, and boutique shops. This walk offers beautiful views and a variety of landscapes, including woodlands and open fields. The area is rich in history and natural beauty.
The
route
Is it dog friendly?
All my walks are dog friendly, however please have your dog under control at all times. There will be livestock on route. Ensure you abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
). Remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the
My First Event
section under "About Us".
Image / photo credits:
All images taken by the event leader (April 2025) with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 12 miles, total ascent is 512 meters. There will be several steep inclines to navigate, surfaces are dirt, grass, flint, chalk and tarmac.

Title: All aboard the Smugglers path, Hastings to Rye
Summary:
From the cobbled heart of Rye, we board a train bound for Hastings, a town steeped in the echoes of 1066. Hastings is famed for the Battle of Hastings, where William the Conqueror defeated King Harold, reshaping England’s future. Today, it retains its fishing fleet—Europe’s largest beach-launched fleet—and offers a lively cultural scene.
Stepping onto the King Charles III England Coast Path, we trace the coastline south. The sea breeze carries stories of smugglers past, while the path is now part of a national initiative to provide an unbroken route along England’s coast. Inland we veer across Winchelsea Marsh, reclaimed land once under the sea, now a haven of grazing sheep and quiet waterways.
Returning to Rye, we are welcomed by a tapestry of medieval lanes and half-timbered houses. The town’s historic pubs—like The Mermaid Inn—still whisper of smugglers known as the Hawkhurst Gang. Sit in these centuries-old taprooms, where ghosts of the past might lean over your shoulder, and tales of clandestine activities drift through the air, blending history and legend in every pint.........
The
Route
Is it dog friendly?
All my walks are dog friendly, however please have your dog under control at all times. There will be livestock on route. Ensure you abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
). Remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the
My First Event
section under "About Us".
Image / photo credits:
All images taken by the event leader (May 2026) with permission for ODL use.

Title: Hope Valley Camp 2026
Summary:
Join us for a weekend of camping and walking in the heart of the Peak District National Park. Hardhurst Farm is a great campsite on a working farm in the popular Hope Valley. There will also be an opportunity to do some
outdoor climbing
at Stanage Edge. This activity is suitable for beginners - no previous climbing experience is necessary. If you would like to take part, please sign up on the
separate event page
, as numbers are limited and the activity will also be open to members who are not attending the camp.
Hope is ideally placed for access to some of the best walking the Peak District has to offer.  Options for walks include: the Great Ridge; Win Hill; Kinder Scout Plateau and Ladybower Reservoir. Choices of walks will depend on leader availability. We will aim to offer at least one walk on Saturday and a shorter walk, possibly ending with a pub lunch, on Sunday.
If you would prefer a more relaxed weekend, you can chill at the campsite, visit some of the nearby villages or take a trip to the caves in Castleton.
The
Travellers Rest Pub
is adjacent to the campsite. They serve great food and some may choose to eat there on Saturday evening.
Campsite Facilities
Facilities on the campsite clean and functional. There are plenty of warm showers. We are currently the only campers staying that weekend so we wont need to share facilities with many others.
For more information on campsite facilities, please review the "What to Bring" section below.
Hope station is only a short walk away, so the site is easily accessible by train from Manchester or Sheffield (see location details below).
Campervans, motorhomes and caravans
Campervans and small motorhomes can be accommodated on the tent camping field. Larger vehicles and caravans cannot be accommodated this time, sorry - the usual campsite area is closed.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are allowed as long as they are kept on a lead and are looked after by their owners at all times.
Photos
: All images provided by the event leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: It can be as easy or difficult as you wish!

Title: Paddle boarding in Bristol Docks
Summary:
Fancy some Stand Up Paddle boarding fun with the backdrop of the beautiful Bristol docks? Then join us at this local charity watersports centre, All Aboard!
All Aboard pride themselves in providing community watersports activities for ALL, so we are delighted to be working with them on this. The session is instructor led, so will be on hand to offer tips, guidance, and encouragement to build your confidence and to make sure you have fun!
No experience necessary and everything required is provided - just turn up with the clothing suggested (see the "what to bring" section down below, and get stuck in and having fun with fellow OutdoorLads.
Our session is for 2 hours - enough time to learn some watersports skills and see the docks without getting too exhausted.
We will go for a (optional) late Sunday lunch afterwards if there is an appetite..
Photos:
Property of OutdoorLads

Title: Reading Sizzling Summer Social
Summary:
Please note that this event is on a
Thursday evening
to avoid any evenings with World Cup matches and the ensuing rowdiness in the town centre and any clash with the Big SE weekend in the New Forest starting on the Friday.
With summer in full swing and the long, light evenings here to enjoy, join us for a relaxed night at a local town centre institution offering cheap and cheerful pies, sides, desserts and beer.
A Bit About The Venue:
Sweeney & Todd is a bit of an institution in Reading, running as a family business for over 40 years. A non-fussy, reasonably priced option, they serve a wide range of home made pies (in excess of 20 options!) with chips and veggies or salad, with a bar selling beer, wine and other options. Vegetarian (but not vegan) pie options are available and they also offer a range of desserts. More details about the venue and menu including the pie options can be found
here
The restaurant closes at 9pm, hence the 7pm start, but for those who wish to stay out later we can move to a pub afterwards, for example reputedly the oldest pub in Reading, the Rising Sun, is just next door.
Why come along?
Come for dinner, stay for the laughs. Whether you're new to OutdoorLads or a regular face, it's a great opportunity to meet new people, catch up with friends, and enjoy a sociable evening together. The only thing you need to bring is yourself — we'll take care of the rest. Just turn up ready for good conversation, plenty of banter, and a welcoming crowd.
🍽️ Tasty pies and drinks
😄 Anticipated friendly mix of familiar faces and new members
☀️ Make the most of a summer evening with great company
🤣 Plenty of laughs, stories, and easy conversation
🏙️ Find out what's coming up locally with OutdoorLads over the summer and beyond

Title: Offa's Dyke Part 18: Fforden to Lower Cwm
Summary:
This is the
eighteenth
in a series of day walks along the Offa's Dyke Path - the idea was to reach the English border in stages, but now we have invaded England. Light the beacons!
This will be a logistically tricky event as we will be parking at the end point, organising the group then driving with half of the cars 15 minutes to the true start point.
After finishing the remaining cars will be used to return the first drivers back to their own vehicles.
After a short drive from our parking spot in Churchstoke to the true starting point in Fforden we will head up the road and hop straight onto the Offa's Dyke Path. Following the Path south east we will huddle the England/Wales border through the farmlands of the Vale of Montgomery.
Passing by the small town of Montgomery to the west the distinctive mound and ditch shape of the original Dyke is very clear to see as we head steadily uphill past Brompton Hall and Mellington Hall, a Grade II listed 19th Century country house turned 4* hotel.
Leaving the Path we will turn east and north to navigate our way across farms and down country lanes to return to our original meeting location at the Churchstoke Shopping Centre.
This walk is roughly
16 km/10 miles
, on footpaths, muddy fields and lots of stiles.
Well-behaved OutdoorDogs are very welcome but please ensure they are adequately prepared for all conditions just as much as you are. Note: Leads will be required in areas with livestock, and we will be crossing awkward stiles frequently.
DOG OWNERS PLEASE NOTE: As this walk involves a short public bus journey this carries a risk to dog owners. The bus operators policy regarding dogs is that they're not allowed on seats (easy enough) but also they can be refused if any passenger on board is afraid of dogs. If you are a dog owner you may wish to message me prior to the event to discuss your options.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help. (
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
)
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do
sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Photos provided by event leader with permission for use on other ODL events.
Difficulty & Distance: This walk is of reasonable length, with several steep ascents and descents, requiring a good level of fitness.

Title: Halloween at Dunster: Castles, Coast, and Celtic Hills
Summary:
Join us for a fantastic, varied Halloween day walk exploring the dramatic landscapes surrounding the historic medieval village of Dunster. This is a relatively short, circular route packed with diverse scenery—taking us from ancient wooded hills and historic settlements to the sweeping Somerset coastline, before tracking the nostalgic West Somerset Railway.
We’ll finish with plenty of time to spare in the afternoon to explore the magnificent Dunster Castle and its gorgeous gardens. Costumes are entirely optional, but a bit of Halloween spirit is highly encouraged!
Event Overview
Date:
Saturday, 31st October 2026
Duration:
Approximately 4 hours (excluding castle visit)
Distance:
Around 8.5 miles (13.5 km)
Difficulty:
Moderate. A few steady climbs into the hills, but well-paced with plenty of flat coastal walking to balance it out.
The Route
We begin our journey in the shadow of Dunster Castle, heading straight out into the atmospheric autumnal canopy of Dunster Park.
The Highs:
We’ll climb steadily up through the woods towards the ancient iron-age hillforts of
Bat’s Castle
and
Black Ball
, offering spectacular, moody views over the surrounding Somerset landscape and the Bristol Channel.
The Valleys & Villages:
Skirting the ridges of Aller Hill and Withycombe Hill, we'll drop down through the quiet combes and charming villages of Withycombe and Carhampton.
The Coast:
From Carhampton, we head north to meet the coast at
Blue Anchor
. Here, we'll grab some fresh sea air and look out across the waves.
The Rails:
The final leg brings us back west, tracking alongside the historic
West Somerset Railway
line and crossing the flat marshlands straight back into Dunster.
Afternoon: Dunster Castle & Gardens
After wrapping up the walk, the afternoon is yours to enjoy. We will head over to Dunster Castle (managed by the National Trust). Perched dramatically on a tor, this ancient fortress turned grand country house is the perfect setting for a Halloween afternoon.
You can wander through the multi-tiered subtropical gardens, explore the historic mill, or head inside the castle to soak up centuries of history (and perhaps a ghost story or two).
> Note: National Trust members get into the castle and gardens for free, so remember to bring your membership card! Non-members can purchase tickets on the day.
A Few Haunted Highlights... 🕷️
Dunster has a reputation for being one of the most active paranormal hotspots in the country—local lore says nearly every historic building in the village has a resident spirit. The castle itself has been investigated by the National Trust for its eerie occurrences. Keep an eye out for:
The Grey Lady:
Dunster’s most famous resident spectre, she is frequently seen gliding majestically down the grand 17th-century oak staircase before completely vanishing into a solid wall.
The Man in Green:
If you pop into the National Trust gift shop (housed in the old 17th-century stable block), you might spot a mysterious figure dressed head-to-toe in green walking calmly through the building before disappearing into thin air.
The Chinking of Coins:
On certain days, the ghostly sound of money being counted echoes near the old keeps. Local legend attributes this to the infamous William de Mohun ("The Scourge of the West"), who minted his own illegal currency here during the medieval Civil War.
The Foot of the Blue Kitchen:
In one of the modernised volunteer areas known as the Blue Kitchen, a shaken worker once reported a completely disembodied, naked human foot appearing out of nowhere on the floor before dissolving.
The Seven-Foot Skeleton:
Deep beneath the Gatehouse dungeon, archaeologists once uncovered the remains of a prisoner standing over seven feet tall, still shackled by his wrists and ankles to the stone wall. While no specific ghost has been spotted, visitors note a distinct drop in temperature, and dogs notoriously refuse to go near the steps leading down to it.
Even on our walk before we reach the castle, keep your ears open—tourists on the nearby hills have previously reported the distinct, booming sound of a massive troop of soldiers marching past them, despite the hillsides being completely empty!
What to Bring
Footwear:
Sturdy walking boots or muddy-trail shoes are highly recommended, as the woodland and hill paths can be damp and slick in late October.
Clothing:
Layer up for autumn weather and bring a reliable waterproof jacket—the coastal stretch can get breezy!
Food & Drink:
Pack a packed lunch, snacks, and plenty of water for the trail. We will find a scenic spot along the hills or coast for a lunch stop.
Money/Card:
For entry to the Castle/Gardens if you aren't an NT member, or for a well-deserved hot drink/pint in Dunster afterwards.
Dogs
Dogs are welcome to join us on this event but we do ask the following:
Please appreciate the fact that not everyone is a dog owner or lover- especially when we stop to eat
Please ensure you adhere to the Countryside Code at all times - see (
Keeping Dogs Under Effective Control)
If your dog is uncontrolled and strays in open land frightening other animals or livestock, the leader is supported by the OutdoorLads board of trustees to ask you to leave the event as this is not acceptable behaviour.
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by OutdoorLads members and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use
Difficulty & Distance: Initial climb out of Dunster onto the ridge, then downhill/flat for the rest of the walk

Title: An afternoon wandering around Winchester
Summary:
Spend an afternoon exploring the historic city of Winchester and the nearby River Itchen and St. Catherine's Hill... with photo ops galore! :)
We'll spend the first part of the event walking around Winchester, seeing the famous sites of the Great Hall, Wolvesey Castle (the Old Bishop's Palace, where we can explore the ruins for free) and the glorious Winchester Cathedral (sadly there won't be time to go in, but lots of time for photos from the outside!). If people wish, we can also have a short bit of free time to explore Winchester market near the cathedral, and the King Alfred statue.
We'll then walk out of town along the River Itchen, passing the Hospital of St. Cross before reaching the historic Hockley viaduct. From there we return to Winchester along the Itchen Navigation but not before climbing St. Catherine's Hill where we can rest and enjoy the views as well as attempting the maze!
We'll end the walk at The Black Boy pub where we can stop for a drink - it is about a further 20 mins walk from there back to Winchester station, which I'd be happy to direct.
Alternatively, stick around for the
Winchester Social
that evening - please book separately if you'd like to attend.
Dogs are welcome to join us on this event but we do ask the following: Please ensure you adhere to the Countryside Code at all times - see
(Keeping Dogs Under Effective Control)
.
If your dog is uncontrolled and strays in open land frightening other animals or livestock, the leader is supported by the OutdoorLads board of trustees to ask you to leave the event as this is not acceptable behaviour.
Thank you to @geekyjames for the use of photos, plus a few of my own @anthonyg86
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 9.6km (6 miles). Terrain is mostly tarmac or gravel. Ascent 92m (302 ft). Although this is a short leisure walk, please be mindful that we will be climbing St Catherine's Hill.

Please join us at our Winchester Social where - by popular demand - we will be visiting
The Queen Inn
, a pub and micro-brewery. Feel free to join us for food or just a drink or two as you please -   here is the
menu
.

Title: Chappel viaduct and surrounding countryside
Summary:
Join me on this 13.5km linear walk through the beautiful Essex countryside and see the victorian wonder of Chappel viaduct. The walk starts at Marks Tey station, from there we will head across country to Chappel viaduct, the UK's largest brick structure. Then we will do a loop towards the ancient Chalkney wood.
We will finish at a gorgeous pub in the village of Chappel itself, that backs onto the viaduct, before heading on a train at the near by station back to Marks Tey. The station itself is home to the East Anglian railway museum for those interested.
There is some country roads but mostly public footpaths. It is important to note that trains back to Marks Tey from Chappel and Wakes Colne station are every 39 minutes past the hour.
Is it dog friendly?
4 legged friends are welcome and are allowed on the boat crossing if you're happy to take them onboard, Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
New Members
- We would like to warmly welcome any newbies looking perhaps to try this as their first Outdoor Lads event. You may find this link useful
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
or if you have If you have any questions then feel free to message either of us.

Robin Lenartowicz - OutdoorLads member who gives permission to OutdoorLads to reuse.

Title: Doncaster Pride 2026 - Social
Summary:
Join us for
Doncaster Pride 2026
and some proper Yorkshire LGBTQ+ fun.
Doncaster Pride is an up-and-coming vibrant celebration of inclusivity, diversity and community spirit.  Having hosted UK Pride in 2024 it continues to grow in popularity year on year.
Although OutdoorLads are not participating in the official parade (beginning 11am from St. Sepulchre Gate), we will meet for a social event at Town Fields where the parade ends.
The Town Fields pride arena offers a lively mix of music and performances on the main stage plus a host of entertainment, bars, food and stalls, creating a welcoming space for everyone to connect with others, enjoy a sense of belonging and experience the joy of a community coming together.
Taking place in August, we can once again hope for a very sunny afternoon!
We will meet at the main entrance at 12.30pm
Feel free to wear your striking red OutdoorLads T-shirts to allow us to be found and to promote the organisation.
Tickets are usually available on the gate, however to avoid disappointment
buy tickets ahead
.
The pride arena closes at 9.30pm. If there is an appetite for more socialising we can look at the options Doncaster city centre has to offer.
Difficulty & Distance: Pride / Social Event

Title: Leave your ego at the door - of Freud's Museum (with lunch)
Summary:
Join Shaun and Chris as we discover the history of psychoanalysis.  This tour of the
Freud Museum
will provide insight into the mind and teachings of the "Father of Psychoanalysis", seeing the family dining room (showcasing photos of Freud's Vienna flat), his study with the original couch, which is apparently one of the most famous pieces of domestic furniture in the world!!
After the ground floor, we can venture upstairs to view the Freud family tree on the landing, before proceeding into Anna Freud's room, where she pioneered the practice of child psychoanalysis.  Next door there is a 20 minute film about the family, with footage from Vienna and London, as well as an interview with the man himself!  There is also an exhibition of works by Leonora Carrington, entitled
The Symptomatic Surreal.
Please note that you will need to book tickets in advance.
Use this
link
and select 11.00 on 08 August.  Admission is £16.90 with donation / £14.90 without.
We will anticipate spending about 75 to 90 minutes exploring the museum before we head off for a rather different early afternoon lunch - Vietnamese!
Pho Ta Finchley Road
serves traditional Vietnames dishes including pho, nom salads, stir frys and vegetarian.  All meals will be paid separately and there is no obligation to join us.
Photos courtesy of
www.pexels.com
and ODL members and used with thanks and permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Just walking through a museum

Title: The Castleton Classic Circular
Summary:
Join Mark for a classic circular hike taking in the very best sights of Castleton!
We will start by walking past the historic remains of Peveril Castle before heading up through the dramatic limestone gorge of Cave Dale. From there, we’ll join the Limestone Way and make our way towards the iconic Great Ridge. We will take in the stunning panoramic views from Mam Tor, follow the ridge along to Hollins Cross, and Lose Hill.
After taking in the scenery, we will head down into Hope and follow a leisurely, picturesque route back to Castleton. To finish off a brilliant day on the hills, we will head to a welcoming local pub for a well-earned drink and the option to grab some food.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.
Image Credits: All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 15 km (9.5 miles) with 600m (2,000 feet) of ascent. Approximately 6 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: HULL PRIDE 2026
Summary:
Welcome to Hull Pride 2026! 🌈
Join Chris and Shaun for a fantastic day out at
Hull Pride 2026
, celebrating the event’s
25th anniversary
!
This is a great opportunity to experience one of Hull’s biggest celebrations, support LGBTQ+ rights, and enjoy a welcoming and inclusive atmosphere. Whether you're attending for the first time or are a regular Pride-goer, everyone is welcome to come along and have a great time.
What to Expect
Watch the
Hull Pride Parade
, which starts at
11:00am
Explore the various Pride activities and entertainment across the city
Stop for tea, coffee, and refreshments during the day

if you have one!

Title: Parkrun - Withernsea Promenade
Summary:
Join Richard and lots of other folk for a 5k run at
Withernsea Promenade - Withernsea
Course Description:
The route consists of 3 laps of Withernsea’s stunning promenade with flat paths
parkrun, is a weekly event that takes place every Saturday at locations across the UK. Whilst I'll be running, you can walk or jog the 5k at your own pace. I've been park running for 5 years and enjoy the positive, welcoming and inclusive atmosphere created at these events. As someone who wouldn't call myself a runner, these events are a great way to motivate you to get up and active on a Saturday morning. There is no time limit and, no one finishes last. The Couch to 5k App can be really useful as you develop your 5k runs.
Following this parkrun I will also be leading a coastal walk at
Spurn point
, which you might like to join me for also
About parkrun
To take part in a parkrun and get your official time,
you will need to have
registered
to receive your all important barcode
.
You will need to bring this barcode with you either printed or on your phone.
Parkrun events are run nationwide and indeed internationally, so once you're signed up you can attend anywhere.  If you don't have a barcode you can still run but won't get a time.
Dogs:
parkrun allow one dog per person on a short lead (not extending).  Running with more than one dog, extending leads or waist leads are not permitted at parkrun events.  As a third party event, OutdoorLads does not have any control over this.
OutdoorLads Running Groups:
If you want to join the Outdoorlads' national group on the parkrun website, on your parkrun account select Profile, then groups and type "Outdoorlads (UK)" and click the Join button.
You may also wish to sign up to the
Strava OutdoorLads running club:
https://www.strava.com/clubs/439735
On occasion, parkruns can be cancelled due to lack of volunteers or adverse weather. Should this happen I will try to email members as soon as I am made aware.
All photos under the creative
commons licence
from
Geograph
Malc McDonald. Also:
Chris Morgan, Arek Adeoye and Huckster - Unsplash; Colin Renshaw.
Difficulty & Distance: The course is run on tarmac paths and grass

Title: Spurn National Nature Reserve
Summary:
Spurn point is Yorkshire's very own Lands End - an iconic and constantly moving peninsula which curves between the North Sea and the Humber Estuary. At over three miles long but as little as 50 metres wide, this landscape is unique and ever-changing. Big skies and ever changing wildlife make the evocative landscape of Spurn one of the Yorkshire's most iconic nature reserves which is cut off from the mainland at high tide.
The walk is 12.5K. We will carefully follow this coastline to the restored lighthouse which is over a century old. There we can enjoy our lunch in isolation with nature. We should also have the opportunity to climb the winding flight of stone steps of the lighthouse for an amazing panoramic birds-eye view of the Spurn (ticket £4). This is an ideal location to spot seals and numerous birdlife, an unspoilt wonder of Yorkshire for us to enjoy before we return safely to the mainland before the tide comes in!
Please note: dogs are not allowed at Spurn, in order to protect the sensitive wildlife and habitats.
Car Parking Costs:
£5 per car for all-day parking. This money goes directly back into managing and protecting Spurn. (Members of Yorkshire Wildlife Trust: free)
In addition to this walk, I will be completing
Withernsea Promenade parkrun
should you wish to join me for this before-hand.
All photos under the creative
commons licence
from
Geograph
. Photo credits: Gerald McKenna, G.Laird, Oscar Taylor, Paul Harrop, Mat Fascione
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is relatively flat, it is exposed to the elements on the coast and is a reasonable distance.

Title: Hike The Cotswold Line Part 2 Kingham To Moreton-In-Marsh
Summary:
Please note that this is a
linear walk
between two stations on the Cotswold Line and works equally well for those travelling by train or by car. If travelling by car, you simply need to take the train one stop at the end of the walk to return to your vehicle.
Last August, I led a linear walk between Kingham and Charlbury, titled
"A Tolkien-Inspired Walk in the Wolds"
. This walk starts from Kingham once again but heads in the opposite (northerly) direction.
Since then, I have discovered that it is possible to create a series of enjoyable day walks along the entire Cotswold Line – the railway linking Oxford and Hereford. These walks would showcase some of the landscapes of the Cotswolds and Malvern Hills, while also passing through attractive villages, historic market towns and cathedral cities, including Oxford, Worcester and Hereford. If there is sufficient interest, I hope to develop this into an occasional series, gradually extending the route along the line from where we began.
This walk takes us through gentle, peaceful countryside, passing picturesque honey-stone villages, with a lunch stop at the historic Chastleton House (National Trust), where there will be time to visit the house and grounds if you wish. The walk finishes in a charming market town.
The sights:
Kingham:
is renowned for its honey-toned stone cottages and tranquil village green. It was named "England’s Favourite Village" by
Country Life
magazine in 2004. We won't see the village centre during this walk, but if you arrive early then feel free to explore (it's over 1 mile away from the station though).
Lower Oddington:
is known for its traditional limestone cottages and the historic St Nicholas Church, which contains remarkable medieval wall paintings including one of Britain’s best-preserved “Doom” paintings.
Adlestrop:
is a tiny, very traditional Cotswold village, best known for inspiring the famous Edward Thomas poem
Adlestrop
, written after a brief train stop there in 1914, which turned the village into a symbol of quiet rural England.
Chastleton House:
is a rare Jacobean country house, largely untouched since the early 17th century. Unlike many stately homes that were heavily remodelled over time, Chastleton survived in an almost time-capsule state, which is what makes it distinctive. Built between 1607 and 1612 for the wealthy wool merchant Walter Jones, the house still contains original furnishings, panelling, tapestries and even graffiti from past centuries. It has a slightly faded, atmospheric quality rather than polished grandeur. The property is now managed by the National Trust, and for those that are members or are happy to pay we can allow people time for a quick visit during the lunch break.
Evenlode:
is a tiny, timeless Cotswold village of honey-coloured stone cottages, ancient farmland and quiet river meadows near the Oxfordshire border.
Moreton-in-Marsh:
is a historic Cotswold market town known for its broad high street, elegant honey-stone buildings and traditional Tuesday market dating back centuries. It combines classic rural charm with independent shops, inns and is one of the Cotswolds’ most accessible and popular small towns.
The Route:
Leaving Kingham station, we briefly follow a B-road before passing signs marking the boundary of Gloucestershire and the Cotswolds. We then cross the River Evenlode and take a footpath into open fields.
The first few miles of the walk are very quiet, pleasant but unremarkable as we make our way across a succession of fields and eventually into woodland. The scenery becomes increasingly rewarding as the day progresses.
After around four miles, we reach our first settlement, the attractive village of Lower Oddington. We first pass its church, which sits apart from the village itself, before continuing into the village with its picturesque cottages and welcoming pub.
Following a short stretch along a main road, we detour into delightful parkland, passing a cricket pitch on our left with an impressive country house visible ahead. We then arrive in Adlestrop, a village full of character and home to a particularly attractive church.
Leaving Adlestrop behind, we begin a long but steady climb through open fields towards Chastleton. Looking back, there are increasingly fine views towards the Cotswold escarpment. At the top of the climb we reach Chastleton House, where I plan to stop for an extended lunch break. There will be plenty of space to enjoy lunch in the parkland, together with the option of visiting the house or enjoying tea and cake at the nearby church.
After lunch, the route descends through rolling farmland to the charming village of Evenlode.
Beyond Evenlode, we continue through a mixture of farmland and woodland, crossing the railway line before taking a direct route towards Moreton-in-Marsh, with the church tower visible on the horizon for much of the approach. On reaching the town, you can either head straight to the station or take advantage of the many inns, cafés and tea shops before returning home.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are some road sections, crossings. stiles and some fields with farm animals (sheep, cows and horses in this area) where they will need to be on a lead. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos taken by the leader 27.5.2026 and permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12.5 miles, paths of grass, dirt and tarmac, fairly flat with just one notable climb up to Chastleton, a total ascent of 200 meters

Title: A Pepperbox Perambulation with a Lover and a Cuckoo
Summary:
Pepperbox Hill is the highest point on the escarpment of Dean Hill that runs between the Test and Avon valleys in the south-east corner of Wiltshire, and on a clear day, it commands extensive views across the Solent and to the Isle of Wight to the south, with the tall spire of Salisbury cathedral to the north-west and Salisbury Plain beyond.
The
route
starts in the tiny hamlet of Hamptworth. We will set off through Langley Wood National Nature Reserve, an extensive ancient broadleaf woodland of international importance which is the northernmost point of the New Forest National Park.
After crossing the A36, we'll continue north through the village of Whiteparish and then out on to the steady climb up to the escarpment of Dean Hill, taking plenty opportunities to pause and enjoy the wide-ranging views opening up behind us over the entire New Forest and beyond to the Solent and the Isle of Wight.
Upon reaching the top, sweeping views to the north and west will open up, with Salisbury cathedral prominent in the near distance and Salisbury Plain beyond. Heading west along the escarpment, we'll reach the highest point at Pepperbox Hill where we'll pause for lunch in the shadow of the Pepperbox aka Eyre's Folly.
The folly is a three-storey hexagonal brick tower, believed to have been built over 400 years ago in 1606 by Giles Eyre who owned the nearby Brickworth estate. Rather than being a true folly with no real purpose, it is widely thought that it was originally built to provide a sheltered viewing stand for the landowners' wives to observe their husbands engaging in the local hunt while sheltered from the rain and mud. In the early 18th century, it is believed that highwaymen would hide out in the tower, and attack passers-by tired out from the climb. The tower was also used as a lookout point by the local Home Guard during World War II.
Continuing on, we'll cross back over the A36 and on to Standlynch Down where the views continue to open up, particularly to the west across the Avon Valley.
The route then gradually descends back down to re-enter the New Forest in the village of Redlynch, and then shortly afterwards on to Lover, which is actually pronounced like Dover! The village post office closed down in 2008, but a temporary office now opens up in the week leading up to Valentine's Day for those wishing to send cards stamped with "Lover".
The final section of the walk returns into woodland around Hamptworth Lodge and leads us back to our start point conveniently located by the lovely Cuckoo Inn where we can have some final refreshments.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed. Note that the route has a prolonged climb with little shelter from the sun and there are very few water features on the entire route, and so it would be inappropriate to brings dogs in hot weather.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
All images used on this page taken by the event leader and permission granted for use by ODL
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 20km (12.5 miles) with 300m (1,000ft) of ascent, with one long sustained climb

Title: Warmington, Edge Hill & Hornton - aka Banburyshire
Summary:
On Sunday 23 October 1642, the Royalist army descended from Edge Hill to begin the first major battle of the English Civil War. The battle was actually fought in the flat fields south-east of Kineton but took its name from the prominent ridge that dominates the local landscape.
This part of the world is known informally as “
Banburyshire
”. It's the immediate catchment area of the old Banbury Cattle Market which was one of the largest livestock markets in Europe until 1998 – and it includes parts of Northamptonshire and Warwickshire as well as north Oxfordshire.
Our 2-counties walk begins in the idyllic village of
Warmington
, which is situated on the slopes of Edge Hill, on the northernmost tip of the
Cotswolds Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty
. It features picturesque ironstone cottages, a large village green and duckpond.
As we leave the village, we also gradually leave the distant roar of the M40 and cross fields where bleating sheep and singing skylarks become our companions.
We climb up onto the escarpment and follow a delightful woodland path down
Jacob's Ladder
and back up into the hamlet of
Edge Hill
. We'll pass
The Castle
– a classic English folly, built in the 1740s. Grade II listed, it includes an octagonal tower which is said to have been inspired by a tower at Warwick Castle. It's operated as an Inn since 1822.
From Edge Hill, we cross fields and, for a while, walk along the county boundary between Warwickshire and Oxfordshire. It's here that we'll break for a picnic lunch before picking up more field paths to take us to Hornton.
Hornton
includes numerous historic houses, with the St. John the Baptist Church dating back to the 12th century, featuring 14th-century wall paintings.
From Hornton we head north to cross back over the border from Oxfordshire into Warwickshire, returning to our starting point in Warmington.
The Plough
in Warmington provides an opportunity for refreshments for those that want to stay on at the end of the walk, and the nearby
National Herb Centre
is open until 5.30pm for anyone wanting to stock up on new plants for the garden!
Dogs are welcome:
Our walk includes village streets and we need to cross a fairly busy B road a couple of times. We may encounter sheep, lambs, cows or horses and there are a few fields where landowner signs request dogs on a lead.
Waiting List:
If there are no places left on this event, please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are very likely to get a place.
Don't waste that space!
If your plans change and you are unable to make the event, please cancel your attendance so that others get the opportunity to sign up and make travel plans.
New Members:
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then please
Message Me
, or go to the
My First Event
section of the website for more information.
Pictures:
All images taken by the Event Leader (April 18, 2026) with permission for
OutdoorLads
to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: 8.4 miles on a mix of good field paths, country roads and village streets. Total ascent: 280m / 920ft.

Title: Yorkshire Three Peaks Hostel 2026
Summary:
If you are a mountain walk leader and are interested in helping us by leading a group on the Three Peaks (or indeed any other sort of walk) please book onto the waiting list and give Neil a message (below), as if we can get more walk capacity we can increase numbers further.  Thank you :)
Join OutdoorLads for an early September hostel break to walk the
Yorkshire Three Peaks!
You've probably heard of the National Three Peaks of Ben Nevis, Snowdon/Yr Wyddfa and Scafell Pike, in many ways more a driving challenge than a walking challenge, and requiring a certain level of insomnia.
But did you know there is also a famous walking challenge in Yorkshire too, walking (approximately) a marathon
over a circuit of Whernside, Ingleborough and Pen y Ghent in the Dales?  It's that challenge we will be undertaking on this hostel weekend, with the added benefit of a comfortable lounge to sit in and a proper (bunk) bed to sleep in afterwards!
Why not do some other OutdoorLads mountain walks to get your fitness up before tackling the challenge?
Don't worry, though, if it turns out you can't make it all the way round, the route takes us past Horton-in-Ribblesdale station where it would be possible to take a train back to Ribblehead, and it'd be possible to bail off after our first mountain, Whernside, and head for the pub.  So if you think you can do it and fancy a go please do sign up!
The Plan
Friday evening
: arrive from 4:30pm (no earlier please), we will have the traditional OutdoorLads buffet and a briefing around 8pm before socialising at the hostel or maybe a visit to the local pub.
Saturday
: we will be up quite early to head out after breakfast onto the main event, the Yorkshire Three Peaks walk, with the aim being to complete it within 12 hours if possible.  When we get back we'll have tea/coffee and cake, and a hearty dinner which I'm sure we will all need by then, then we can socialise in the hostel, pop out to the pub or if you're really worn out maybe an early night - or perhaps bring your favourite board game if that's your thing?
Sunday
: after breakfast it'll be time to pack, sort out the hostel and say our goodbyes.  I don't plan as things stand to offer a Sunday walk (we'll all be tired!) but you might want to do some touristing around the local area before heading home?
Sponsorship?
Unlike the Keswick to Barrow walk which OutdoorLads also takes part in as a fundraiser, this isn't specifically being run as a sponsored event, but if you fancy doing some fundraising yourself as part of this walk, either for OutdoorLads or for another charity of your choice, please feel free (and thanks in advance if you do decide to support OutdoorLads!)
Other Activities
While the main intention of this hostel is to complete the Three Peaks of Yorkshire walk, if other mountain or lowland leaders join the event it may be possible to offer shorter walks as well.  If you are such a leader, please send me a message via my profile below to let me know what you are interested in offering and I will note it on here.  Thank you :)
It would also be possible to stay with us in the hostel and do your own thing if you're a cyclist or just want to "train geek out" and have a ride on the scenic Settle-Carlisle Line (the town of Settle is said to be a nice day out, as is the city of Carlisle), take some photos of the famous Ribblehead Viaduct, have a meal and a pint at the Station Inn or just chill at the hostel!  If this is your intention please send me a message when you've booked on so I know I don't need to account for you on the main walk.
Dogs
Regrettably this event is not suitable for dogs - the walk is probably too long for most and they are not allowed in the hostel by the owners.  Sorry!
Photo Credits
Ribblehead Viaduct and Pen y Ghent by Kreuzschnabel, CC BY-SA 3.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Blea Moor Road at Gearstones Lodge by David Dixon, CC BY-SA 2.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Junction of Pennine Way and Yorkshire Three Peaks Path by Roger Templeman, CC BY-SA 2.0 <
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0
>, via Wikimedia Commons
Yorkshire Three Peaks sign - Dave Morton, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
Difficulty & Distance: 43km/26.7 miles (a marathon!), 1729m/5670ft height gain

Title: Tom a' Chòinich & Toll Creagach, Glen Affric
Summary:
A duo of Munros in arguably the most beautiful glen of all, Affric!
These are two very different but neighbouring Munros closer to the entrance (and thus the car parks) in Glen Affric, but nevertheless they are still a challenging day out.
Tom a' Chòinich has rocky ridges and an appealing peak, Toll Creagach is a more rounded, mossy summit. The former is 1112m, the latter 1054m.
Our route is 16.5km or 10.25miles. Some of the route involves boggy ground and crossing a couple of burns (if in spate, the walk will be altered). Total ascent 1096m.
Dogs are welcome but must be kept under close control.
Photos are taken by AMD and are used with his gracious permission.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our Telegram
chat
and
notification
groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: Remote, pathless in places, boggy in places, two streams to cross, 1096 m of ascent.

Title: See Loch Ness From Above
Summary:
Probably the most famous stretch of water in the world.
If you've not yet seen the beloved Nessie, maybe viewing her home from above will improve your chances!! If you don't know what to look for, shots of Nessie and her offspring are included in the photos on this page......
Loch Ness, as well as being home to Nessie, has a few other impressive facts about it (yes, Nessie's residence in the Loch is a fact!!) Loch Ness contains more water that all the rivers and lakes of England combined. It is far deeper than any part of the vast North Sea.
Enough marketing, let's get on with our day out.
We'll be seeing Loch Ness from above by climbing up a lovely hill called Meall Fuar-mhonaidh. That's easy for you to say. It stands proud at 699m above sea level.
The walk is out and back, 9km / 6miles and takes in some beautiful woodland before ascending slopes on open hillside. There are a few short, steep sections, but nothing of any real challenge. The walk is suitable for any reasonably active hill walker.
From the summit, if conditions allow, we should be able to see almost the full length of Loch Ness, with just a litre or two towards Inverness obscured by the hills. In the other direction, way beyond Loch Ness in the Great Glen, we should see Ben Nevis towering above Fort William. In other directions, we get a glimpse of seemingly endless summits in the Highlands. Finally, with a stroke of luck, we might see Nessie in her waters or sunning herself on a remote lochside beach.
Dogs are welcome on this walk, kept under close control.
Photos:
taken by Rob Mitchell and used with permission.

Title: Woburn Estate
Summary:
Join David and Alex for varied circular route through the Woburn Estate and surrounding countryside. We’ll start from the main car park opposite St Mary’s Church and head out through the Deer Park, skirting lakes and woodland and keeping an eye out for black squirrels and deer along the way.
We’ll follow a mix of public footpaths, estate tracks and quiet lanes, including sections alongside the safari park fences - so there’s a good chance of seeing wildlife, even if the lions stay put. The route leaves the Woburn Estate and takes us through villages like Eversholt and Milton Bryan, where there’s an optional pub stop near the halfway point.
Later on we’ll pass some interesting historical spots, including a former WWII propaganda site built in 1942 as a studio for the Political Warfare Executive, and Paris House (which a previous Duke of Bedford brought back from the Paris Exhibition of 1878), before looping back through the estate past ponds and open grassland to finish where we started.
It’s mostly gentle walking with a few steady rises, nothing technical, and we’ll keep a relaxed pace with stops as needed. A short section may involve paddle along a stream, which can be avoided if not everyone is wearing waterproof footwear!
There are warning signs in the Woburn estate as the large number of deer means there may be ticks present, so please be aware. The grass us usually grazed short so usually only a problem if picnicking!
Don't waste that space!
Summer means lots of members looking to get out more so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
The walk is not suitable for dogs as it crosses a deer park and the edge of a safari park.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse
Difficulty & Distance: Mostly gentle walking. Around 4 hours walking time.

Title: From Harbour to Shore - a Wittering Walk to Adore
Summary:
Only five people attended this walk when I ran it before, so perhaps I can get away with repeating it to plug a gap in the southeast walk schedules, and more guys will find it a novel experience. Starting in Chichester, we'll follow the calm, tree-lined Chichester Canal south until it opens into Chichester Harbour, sparkling, spacious and special. We'll stroll past Birdham’s marinas and along Itchenor’s winding lanes, with seabirds calling overhead and sails on the horizon. We'll end our journey on the golden sands of West Wittering. Fresh air, fine views, and a frolic on the sand on one of the best beaches around, and as this is a linear walk, a spell relaxing on the bus back.
The sights:
Birdham:
Some pleasant houses, especially the Westlands Estate. Birdham Pool was a tidal mill pond but became one of the first public marinas in the country in 1946. Chichester Marina is the chief attraction: a big rectangular harbour with sleek yachts along pontoons, created in 1966. St James' Church is big and bulky C14 but harshly restored in 1882.
Chichester Harbour AONB:
'Chichester Harbour is one of the few remaining undeveloped coastal areas in Southern England...Its wide expanses and intricate creeks are at the same time a major wildlife haven and among some of Britain's most popular boating waters. The massive stretch of tidal flats and saltings are of outstanding ecological significance.' (From Wikipedia)
West Itchenor:
'One short, nicely unpretentious street of cottages, mostly 18th century, running down to the water's edge.' (Ian Nairn,
The Buildings of England: Sussex
). Huge waterside properties all around. From 1744 to the early C19 warships where intermittently made there.
West Wittering:
An attractive and exclusive village with large thatched houses and villas set in large gardens. Cakeham Manor was once a palace of the bishops of Chichester, now an attractive group of medieval, Tudor and Georgian parts. The tall brick tower of the early C16 stands out amid the flat fields.
East Head
and West Wittering Beach:
'One of the last surviving pieces of natural coastline in West Sussex. East Head is an example of the nationally rare and fragile, yet dynamic sand-dune habitat. Walk, play and sunbathe on the beach, which is considered one of the best in Sussex [it has Blue Flag status (see the
website
)].' (From the National Trust website). The car park and beach get horribly busy in the summer, but our walk beforehand will be relatively quiet.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them. Dogs are excluded from the main swimming beach at West Wittering but are allowed nearby. There are dog waste bins. Warm weather could be exhausting for dogs although we are close to water most of the way. Any dog off its lead must be under control.
The
route
(please click on the link to see it)
:
Starting at Chichester Railway Station, we'll follow the New Lipchis Way all the way along the canal to the beach. After the beach, we'll walk to Cakeham Road to catch the bus back to Chichester.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance. The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby and the beach retains its Blue Flag award every year. Lifeguards are in attendance at the beach.

Photo

Simon Carey
(
cc-by-sa/2.0

David Dixon
(
cc-by-sa/2.0

CC-SA-BY2.0
. The other pictures were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance: 18 km (11.5 miles); terrain: almost flat and on beach shingle, grass, dirt, gravel, sand and tarmac; total climb is just 14m.

Title: Hereford and Breinton Springs
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow and largely flat 10km / 6 mile walk from Hereford Cathedral to the National Trust site of
Breinton Springs
.
This short walk starts and finishes in the cathedral grounds in Hereford and takes a leisurely stroll along the river Wye.
Along the way you get some stunning views of the cathedral and the river before ending up in a peaceful churchyard surrounded by ancient orchards and the possibility of coming face to face with some endangered wildlife in an SSSI. We also walk past the spot where a dog fell into the Wye more than a hundred years ago, inspiring Elgar to write one of the
Enigma Variations
. On our return to the city we take a loop through the site where
Hereford Castle
once stood.
The walk is planned for the end of September, so if you wished to stay in Hereford until the following day, you could join the
Queers & Beers monthly meet up
, which normally takes place on the last Sunday of the month. And if you were to arrive early, you could pop to the café in All Saints Church to check out a
particularly rude wood carving
.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

Summary:

Reading pride are increasing the size and focus for their dedicated Sports & Outdoor activities zone within the pride event park.  So, whether you like climbing, bouldering, hostelling, cycling, camping, canoeing or day walks grab your OutdoorLads RED T-shirt and come help us encourage new members and help show Reading that OutdoorLads does #GetOutMore!
As part of Reading's commitment to helping the community get outside for their mental and physical well being we will have a stall in the Sports & Outdoor zone and this gives OutdoorLads the chance to engage with potential new members.  We are looking for 6 ODLer's to help cover the stall.
Reading is easily accessible so why not make a mini break of it and join us for a variety of events over the weekend!

free,

Title: Midweek Meander - Iron Giant Meatballs
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow walk in Derbyshire, taking in the Iron Giant and ending up in the IKEA restaurant.
The Bennerley Viaduct, known informally as the Iron Giant, and occasionally described as
Derbyshire's Eiffel Tower
is a wrought iron structure that once carried a railway. It proved impossible to demolish, got listed in the 1970s and recently has been cared for by a friends group who have worked hard to get a decent footpath over the top of it.
IKEA
opened in Nottinghamshire in 1997
and revolutionised home furnishings in the East Midlands. Their low priced café is where we will finish our walk and while they are most famous for their meatballs, their menu includes veggie options. Signing up for an
IKEA Family card
in advance might give you a deal in the restaurant.
This walk includes a Nottinghamshire furniture giant and Derbyshire iron giant. The boundary between the two counties is the River Erewash which we will cross.
If this is your first ODL event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the ODL website.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

All images provided by the walk leader and permission is granted to ODL for their use.
Difficulty & Distance: This is largely flat with less than 100m of ascent, but some ramps up to the viaduct.

Title: Board Game Cafe - Birmingham
Summary:
An evening of board games, food, drink and probably polite company.  Join Outdoorlads for an evening at the popular board game cafe in Birmingham.
There's a small cover charge of £2.50 per person, per hour. This is paid when the leader books the table, so expect a message the day before to confirm your attendnace.  We will likely play in small groups of 5.
If the event is full, join the waiting list and I'll book a additional tables if there is enough interest.

Title: Highland Coast Kayak and Wild Camping Expedition 2026
Summary:
Now a regular expedition in our Scottish calendar, join us and local guide/antarctic kayak expert
Will Copestake
on a 3 day guided, and catered, kayak tour of Scotland's beautiful northwest coast!
This year we’re developing a “North West Explorer” style trip. Which is essentially a 'wherever the weather and tides make sense’ package somewhere between Gairloch and the North Coast, within easy reach of Ullapool - for example we may do Kylesku to Oldany over three days (a fab trip), or Summer Isles, or south toward Rhuba Reigh - there is a large area to go at and the weather will make the final call.
Wherever we choose, we will venture from our start point to seek caves, archways and sandy beaches. We should see seals and possibly porpoise and dolphins. We will take the days at our own pace - you do not need to be an experienced kayaker or even a strong swimmer. As part of the itinerary, we will take regular pauses on wild beaches and offer the chance to brew a hot drink on a wood fired kelly kettle stove.
In the evenings, we will camp on the beach and enjoy a catered meal with a glass of wine - kayaks give us the chance to carry a little bit of luxury into the islands.
Itinerary
Friday 14th Aug - Breakfast at your own accommodation - 10am - meet guide at launch site (tbc), who will give out boats and equipment, get paddling, camp on beach with catered meal and wine.
Saturday 15th Aug - Breakfast at camp before more paddling, lunch on beach, paddle camp on beach with catered meal and wine.
Sunday 16th Aug - Breakfast at camp, paddle, lunch, paddle, return to shore for 3pm.
There is lots of accommodation in the general area and we will coordinate over the coming weeks. The event leader will be staying at a local campsite the night before the event (details below) and you will be welcome to join, but you will be responsible for making your own arrangements. There may be opportunity to add some days on to the end of the trip to explore the area further on foot so, if you are planning to extend your trip, do share plans with the group!
How to Book:
The cost of this fantastic expedition is
£465 for guided paddling, equipment, and food for three days
. You will need to book directly with the provider by emailing
will@kayaksummerisles.com
and quoting "Outdoorlads" - The guide's website is
here
. The OutdoorLads refund policy does not apply - terms and conditions are as per the activity provider.
Please be aware
that it is the paying of the event fee (or deposit if agreed by the provider) that secures your place on the trip, rather than signing onto the trip via the OutdoorLads site. Where deposits are accepted, balance payments will be requested by the provider prior to August 2026.
If a waitlist builds up, the provider may be able to Co-opt an additional operator and boats so it may be worth signing up!

Title: FRIDAY FELLS - High Cup Nick
Summary:
Join Friday Fells for an 8.5 mile circular mountain walk (575m ascent) in the North Pennines National Landscape - a great way to start your weekend early.
This walk takes in the spectacular geological feature of High Cup Nick and Murton Pike with spectacular views over the Eden Valley.
We'll start the walk from the village of Murton.  We cross over to High Cup Gill and follow the valley its narrow head.  It's a short scramble up to the rim, which we'll follow for a while, taking in the views all the while.  Eventually, we'll cross to Murton Pike (594m), a fantastic viewpoint.  From there, it's a short descent to the village and the car.
At the end of the day, we'll go for an optional drink at the
The Stag Inn
in Dufton.
You can find the planned route
here
, though we may vary this depending on the abilities and ambitions of the group and weather conditions.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
New Members:
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event, do note that this walk is likely to require some experience. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Well-trained dogs are welcome but please note and abide by the
Countryside Code
. Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.

/
photo
credits
:  All images (c) JPV with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: For a mountain walk, a modest amount of ascent and distance

Title: Glasgow evening walk - Carnbooth AA Battery
Summary:
Glasgow evening walk - Carnbooth AA Battery
An evening walk from Queens park to Linn park and Carnbooth AA Battery.
This former World War II artillery defense site was built to guard the industrial city of Glasgow. This was one of at least 43 similar sites around the city constructed to intercept any incoming air attack from German forces on industrial sites across the banks of the River Clyde.
A list of these sites and locations can be found here:
https://www.secretscotland.org.uk/index.php/Secrets/AABatteriesClyde
The battery site originally held four gun emplacements, two magazine buildings, a command post and a gun store. Interestingly, this site was later modified during the Cold War to counter Soviet threats. It was during this period that some of the buildings were demolished and a command post, engine house, and a perimeter security fence were constructed. The bolts from the metal structures of the anti-aircraft artillery still remain on the floor of each gun base - see photo.
This is primarily an evening walk through the greener parts of the south side. The ruins at the end are an interesting bonus.
Please don't expect The Guns of Navarone !

taken by Brendan70
