Title: Yeavering Hell!
Summary:
A 6.5 mile very hilly walk to Northumberland’s finest Hill Fort. This route takes in two peaks with outstanding views with a slow 3 mile walk up.
Yeavering Bell is probably the leader's favourite Northumberland inland walk but only if stop for a couple of breaks along the way. On the last venture out the leader saw a couple of adders but no wild mountain goats which was a shame.
Although nowhere near as high as The Cheviot it feels like it after the effort of the ascent. The route is a challenging walk and is rewarding with a lot to see.
Image credits:
Images provided by event leader and co-leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them
Difficulty & Distance: Steep climbs first part of the walk. Rocky walk getting back.

Title: BIG Peaky Weekender 2026 (Hostel)
Summary:
🏔️ BIG Peaky Weekender: The BIG20 Edition 🏔️
Some events are just instant classics. Born during our 15th Anniversary celebrations, the
BIG Peaky Weekender
has quickly become a legendary staple of the OutdoorLads calendar. Now, as we hit our
BIG20
, we’re heading back to the heart of the Peak District for a celebration of where we’ve been and where we’re going!
⛺ Choose Your Base Camp
We have exclusive use of this fantastic outdoor centre. Whether you’re a hostel regular or a hardcore camper, we’ve got a spot for you:
Hostel
Camping
Teepee Camping Add-on
🥾 Getting Out More
This site is perfectly located for everything we love: walking, cycling, mountain biking, climbing, and paddlesports. Since it joined our calendar five years ago, it’s become a member favorite for its "ace in every way" location.
On Saturday, our Leaders will be out in force. Expect a "Greatest Hits" selection of hikes, rides, and local adventures. On Sunday there will be a small selection of events for before we head back home. We'll list the event pages below when we have them. If you're a Leader and keen to lead something, get in touch!
🍻 The BIG20 Social: Community & Connection
While other BIG events can be quite high energy, BIG Peaky stays true to its roots as a relaxed, social homecoming.
Our legendary bar-in-a-teepee is back for the BIG20! This is about getting out more and catching up with one another. No late-night disco—just a huge, welcoming space to chat, catch up with old friends, make new ones, and maybe get a bit competitive over some board games.
🍔 Fuelling the Anniversary
Look out for meal booking pages in the weeks before the event. You can pre-book Friday and Saturday evening meals, breakfasts, and packed lunches.
Hostel attendees have the option of catering for themselves in the hostel kitchen/dining facilities. However, you must tidy up everything after yourself as if you were never there. Like any other event, we do not have cleaning staff, and we rely on and expect attendees to clean up after themselves.
✨ Be Part of the Story
From its birth at our 15th anniversary to this massive 20th celebration, the BIG Peaky Weekender is all about the people. Whether you’ve been with us for twenty years or twenty minutes, you are part of the legend.
Don’t miss out on this chapter of our BIG20 year. Grab your spot now!
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs - they are not allowed on site.
Campervans, caravans, motorhomes:
Due to the venues restrictions, we are unable to accept campervans, caravans and motorhomes.
Images:
Aerial shots kindly supplied by and used with permission from Will Allaway. Other photos taken by and used with permission from OutdoorLads Members and photographer William Hickey.
Difficulty & Distance: As much or a little as you please!

Title: New Date: Wind In The Willows River Summer 😎 Sunday Saunter
Summary:
Walk Rescheduled From Sunday 12th July To Sunday 23rd Aug Due To Prolonged Heatwave Conditions.
Join us for a relaxed and sociable 10-mile circular walk through some of the most quintessentially English stretches of the Thames Valley – with one cheeky hill to earn your lunch. This is classic
“Wind in the Willows” country
. The Wind in the Willows was inspired by this very stretch of river, where Kenneth Grahame spent part of his childhood in Cookham. As we walk beside the Thames, it’s not hard to imagine Ratty and Mole “messing about in boats” along these same waters. Think lazy riverbanks, boats drifting by, and just enough charm to make you feel like you should be carrying a picnic hamper (or at least a decent snack).
The route is 10 miles, however there is the option just to do the morning section (just over 5 miles) and take the train back from Marlow Station.
The Route:
Starting at Cookham Station, we will initially walk into the older centre of the village, past the Stanley Spencer Gallery. We will then walk through the churchyard of the scenic Holy Trinity Church and then join the Thames Path, following the river for about 1.5 miles.
Later we will diverge from the Thames Path walking uphill towards Winter Hill where we are rewarded with sweeping views across the Thames Valley. We will follow the ridge for some time before climbing back down and then taking a flatter route towards Marlow. Entering Marlow, we will walk over it's historic bridge (see more details below) before getting to it's lively High Street.
At lunch we will take an extended break enabling participants to do their own thing, whether grabbing a coffee and sandwich in one of the cafes or a picnic lunch in the riverside park.
After lunch we will re-join the Thames path, which is now on the North side of the river, and walk along it for 3 miles towards Bourne End with it's marina. At the railway bridge we cross back to the South side of the river, re-trace some of our steps from the morning walk and then take a path back towards Cookham Station following the railway line across fields and later passing by a golf course.
Depending on the time that we arrive back, mindful that for those taking the train services are just hourly, there may be time for a quick drink or tea and cake at the nearby cafe (which closes at 4pm on Sundays).
The Sites:
Cookham:
is a quintessential Thames-side village, widely regarded as one of the most picturesque in England. The village has a strong artistic identity, most notably through its association with Stanley Spencer, whose work vividly depicted local life; and his legacy is preserved in the Stanley Spencer Gallery.
Winter Hill:
located just above Cookham, is characterised by its elevated position and sweeping views across the Thames Valley and the Chilterns. Much if it is protected by the National trust.
Marlow:
is a lively and attractive Georgian town that blends historic character with a strong leisure and dining scene. The town’s elegant suspension bridge, designed by William Tierney Clark, is a notable landmark and the famous Chain Bridge in Budapest, designed by the same architect, is a larger scale replica of Marlow Bridge.
Bourne End:
is a village where the Thames meets the River Wye and has a marina and railway bridge which we will pass and cross. The area also played a role in early British film production at Riverside Studios.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, the sections along the Thames Path can get busy, the lunch spot in Marlow can get very busy and there are sections walking along pavements alongside busy roads. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos:
All photos the leader's own, with permission given to OutdoorLads for use.
Difficulty & Distance: 10 miles, relaxed moderate pace, mainly flat with just one hilly section around Winter Hill, total ascent 110 meters.

Title: Northern Ireland's Causeway Coast
Summary:
We're off over the Irish Sea for four nights to explore the Causeway Coast.
Our hostel has a sea view and is in an ideal location for seeing the various attractions of the area - top of the list is the Causeway Coast itself with its hexagonal basalt columns, this being part of a fine coastal walk. Also within walking distance of the hostel is Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge, perhaps not for those with a fear of heights as it hangs 30m above the sea!
A short drive away is Ballycastle with the historic Bonamargy Friary and it's harbour, this being the port for the short sailing to Rathlin Island - an island six miles long with tranquil walking trails and views back across the causeway coast. If we go a little further east we find ourselves in the Glens of Antrim, an area of ancient woodlands and waterfalls with several beautiful walking trails.
There is lots more to see and do, but why not just come along and discover this stunning part of Northern Ireland....
Our hostel has lots of social space, free table tennis & pool and clean, comfortable dorm rooms - plus a pub 100m away. It's absolutely perfect as a base to explore the local area and this stunning coastline.
For some travel guidance, do check the information provided below the location map.
Dogs:
This venue isn't suitable for dogs.

Title: Summer Days in the Cairngorms
Summary:
Join us for three nights in the western Cairngorms
The plan for this weekend is to have a couple of big hill days in the area, there are
no low level options planned for this event
.
We have the western Cairngorm plateau that comes off the back of Braigh Riabhach with a couple of hills around the remote and little visited Glen Eidart. Also to our west is the Monadhliath, a lesser explored area but if you're bagging the munros there are four over there to be climbed. Plus it's not too far to drive to pick up other hills - Creag Meagaidh, the Laggan hills and as for Corbetts...!  Not expecting any snow to be left this summer, but the photos here were taken in June 2021.
Our base for the weekend is a Mountaineering Scotland hut at the top of Glen Feshie, simple but clean and comfortable accommodation and is a great location for getting up the hills that make the upper Speyside area. The hut has a mix of bunk beds and an alpine sleeping room, mattresses are provided but you will need to bring bedding.

Title: BIG20: North Yorkshire Coastal Weekend
Summary:
BIG20 events are our programme to celebrate our 20th birthday year
- showcasing some of the best and most loved locations and venues we've been to in the last 20 years! We are returning again to one of our favourite hostels in the quiet and quaint village of Robin Hoods Bay, a small fishing village, surrounded by cliffs, 8km south of Whitby. The village, which consists of a maze of tiny streets, has a tradition of smuggling and there is reputed to be a network of subterranean passageways linking the houses. Situated at the end of Wainwright's Coast to Coast path and also on the Cleveland Way, the village is ideally located for coastal and moorland walks and lies within the North York Moors National Park.
Our accommodation is an independent hostel high up on the cliffs overlooking the village.
Itinerary
On Friday evening, there will be a briefing followed by the usual buffet - please bring a suitable food item.
A light breakfast will be provided on Saturday morning, following which there will be at least one walk. We may be able to offer more than one walk option, depending on leader availability. After the walk, there will be an opportunity to relax, explore the village and visit some of the excellent pubs.
On Saturday evening a meal will be provided in the hostel.
Following breakfast on Sunday morning, once we have cleaned and vacated the hostel, a shorter walk will be available for those who don't need to dash off.
Image Credits:
Provided by the hostel venue and OutdoorLads members. Used by OutdoorLads with full permission.
Dogs

Title: Marshes & Meadows: Parkgate to Neston
Summary:
Hi all,
Join Jay as we explore another beautiful corner of the Wirral.
This relaxed, mostly flat walk is perfect if you’re new to OutdoorLads, or simply looking to meet new people and make friends in an easy-going setting.
We’ll start by heading along a scenic path towards Parkgate, with the option to grab an ice cream along the way. From there, we continue along the King Charles III England Coast Path towards Gayton, before looping back via Backwood Hall. We’ll then join the Wirral Way for a stretch, before making our way back to the cars.
To round off the day, we’ll stop at Nets Café for a well-earned cuppa and cake, just a short distance from where we finish.
Distance:
19 km (11 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.
New Members:
Please feel free to sign up to this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to message me, or visit the “My First Event” section under “About Us”.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event

Title: Inverbervie and Johnshaven Circular Walk
Summary:
Circular walk along the Aberdeenshire coast with inland return
Inverbervie is the birthplace of Hercules Linton, the designer of the famous tea clipper Cutty Sark. We head down to the beach and trace the coastline south to the fishing villages of Gourdon and Johnshaven. We then head slightly inland to Mill of Benholm and return to Inverbervie by an old coaching route with good views of the sea from the highest point of the walk at Gourdon Hill (136 metres). The route is by paths, old railway line and sections of beach. May be muddy in places.
Transport
Car parking (free) is available in the square and the Stagecoach X7 Coastrider bus service between Dundee and Aberdeen stops at the square.

Title: Settle to Stainforth ft. caves and waterfalls…
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a 9-mile circular walk from Settle to Stainforth in North Yorkshire. We will start our journey through the quaint market town centre before an uphill ascent via the Dales Highway, with spectacular views of the surrounding countryside and town below.
Our route will then take us through Attermire Scar; here we will pass the larger Victoria Cave and smaller Jubilee Cave, carved into the limestone cliff. Victoria cave was discovered in 1837 during the reign of Queen Victoria, hence the name. It is considered an important archaeological site due to the discovery of animal bones up to 130,000 years old! There was also the discovery of 11,000-year-old tools and artefacts, providing the first evidence of human settlement in the area.
On our descent into Stainforth we will pass Catrigg Force, a waterfall cascading over a deep gorge. The beautiful village of Stainforth awaits; we will stop for lunch here after crossing some stepping stones over Stainforth Beck. After lunch, we will pass another waterfall, Stainforth Force, before taking a gentle stroll along the bank of the River Ribble leading us all the way back to Settle. There will be an opportunity to stop for a drink at one of the local establishments in Settle at the end of the walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
All photos taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is approximately 9-miles long through mixed terrain including a couple of steep uphill climbs. It can be quite uneven and rocky underfoot in places.

Title: Birmingham Canals and Edgbaston Resevoir
Summary:
Easy 8 mile circular walk from central Birmingham onto the Birmingham canal old line at Brindley Place before making our way to Edgbaston Reservoir. Edgbaston Reservoir, originally known as Rotton Park Reservoir and referred to in some early maps as Rock Pool Reservoir is a canal feeder reservoir maintained by the Canal and River Trust. This will be our lunch stop before following a hidden disused railway line to Harborne before joining the Worcester and Birmingham canal back to the city via the Mail Box.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk.
There will be opportunity for a coffee or pint afterwards.

Title: Gran Canaria Adventure Week 2027
Summary:
The last four years have proven to be hugely successful and popular...
so we're doing all it all again in 2027!!!
Gran Canaria Adventure Week
is back again for
January 2027
. We have some of what you've loved from previous years, plus new events and ideas to give you an even better winter sun experience.
Feedback from previous years:
"The socials were well organised and the group events were excellent"
"Loved the ability to do as much or as little as we wanted throughout the entire week"
"A fantastic event with something for everyone"
"Good range of activities to keep everyone happy, from leisure city tours to high octane hikes, canyoning, via ferrata - something for everyone! And the organisers are friendly and welcoming, feels like family which is nice"
"All the event leaders were great and super friendly and easy to talk to and ask questions"
Think you know Gran Canaria? Think again!
Let your next (or first!) visit to Gran Canaria be one filled with outdoor adventures. While the island is known as a year-round party destination for LGBTQ+ visitors, this trip is an opportunity to experience something different:
take part in outdoor activities; experience the beauty of the "real" Gran Canaria; and meet friends from around the world.
We are, once again, partnering with
CanCanaria
to bring you a full week of fun and adventurous activities. CanCanaria is a gay-owned and run company based on Gran Canaria, using their extensive local knowledge and the island's magnificent natural beauty to create a programme of excursions and trips that'll leave you with unforgettable memories and stories to tell. At the helm is former OutdoorLads' Chairman Patrick, so you know you will be in good and welcoming hands. Patrick and his team use trusted local volunteers, guides and instructors who are LGBTQ+ friendly, and who hold appropriate insurance and qualifications where necessary.
The
CanCanaria Adventure Week
is festival dedicated to outdoor activities, combining adventures with a holiday in the sun and sociable evenings. Expect
Hiking, Climbing, Sailing, Surfing and Scuba Diving alongside tourist trips, socials, restaurants, bars, a pool party and boat trips.
How do I sign up?
Sign up to this OutdoorLads event
in the usual way. This doesn't get you a place on the CanCanaria event, but it will enable us to see how many of our members are attending, make arrangements together and share details and information easily in the run up to the trip.
Sign up to the CanCanaria Activity Week
on their website
by following this link
. This step is free and simply gets you a place on the overall event. It will also enable CanCanaria to contact you with details of the activities as they become available. You'll be asked to indicate the types of activities you are interested in, and the organisers will then use that information to formulate the final programme.
Sign up to whatever combination of activities and parties you fancy via the pages which CanCanaria will publish in due course
. If you've signed up at Step 2, you will also be notified as the various activities get arranged.
***VIP Offer***
CanCanaria have put together a VIP package. For a fixed price, you get:
50 euro voucher to spend on Adventure Week activities,
only for orders made before September 1st 2026
VIP Breakfast/Brunch
- only available to those with VIP Tickets (10:00 - 12:00: Wednesday 27 January)
Free Entry To:
The Welcome Party, (Monday 25 January)
The Main Pool Party, BBQ included (Wednesday 27 January)
The Sundown BBQ, men only event - BBQ included (Friday 28 January)
Office Adventure Week 2027 T-shirt
One CanCanaria Passport
- a book of discount vouchers to use in bars, shops and restaurants in Maspalomas
Those of us who have attended in previous years have had an amazing trip. There has been a great range of activities which really showed off the beauty of the island, and the evening socials (in a different bar each night) meant there was a regular opportunity to catch up with everyone, get to know your fellow travellers and ask any questions of the events team.
Check out some of the photos on this page which are from previous years!
It is entirely up to you whether you come to Gran Canaria for some or all of the week, or indeed whether you arrive earlier or leave later to make it part of a longer trip.
Here is the Program of Events for 2027 so far, with links to the activity pages where they are ready for booking:

Accommodation
Gran Canaria has accommodation to suit a variety of budgets, from basic self-catering apartments, to full service hotels and villas.
You are welcome to message the event leader or Patrick at CanCanaria for accommodation ideas, and to use the event page comments to arrange sharing. In due course we will also set up an OutdoorLads Telegram group too.
Flights & Transfers
Gran Canaria is easily accessible from various UK airports, with a flight time of around 4 hours. The transfer from the airport to Playa del Ingles is straight forward, either by public bus, taxi or pre-arranged holiday transfer service.
Please note: This is not an OutdoorLads event and is arranged by an external organisation, CanCanaria. The OutdoorLads refund policy does
not
apply and your contract is with Cancanaria. No insurance is provided by OutdoorLads. This is simply an event page to enable members to co-ordinate their holiday and activities together.

Title: Let's go roamin' among Roman ruins (and a pub lunch)
Summary:
Starting from St Albans City station, we will enjoy this 8.50 mile bimble exploring
Roman Verulamium
.  We will head through a small part of town, then amble alongside the River Ver on the Hertfordshire Way.  Much of our path will be on the grounds of the Gorhambury Estate, which is full of Roman, Elizabethan and 18th century history.  We will pass the Roman Theatre of Verulamium (built circa AD 140), pass Gorhambury House on our way to
Old Gorhambury House
, a Grade I listed manor house, built between 1563-1568.  This English Heritage site is free to enter and we will allow time to explore the ruins.
Just after 4.5 miles in, we will stop for a wonderful pub lunch at
The Holly Bush
, located in the leafy village of Potters Crouch.  As of the date of publishing, I am working on the pre-order menu with the pub and that will be shared later.  Expect slightly upscale pub grub, with the likes of pave of pork belly, pappardelle beef ragu, lamb rump and artisanal pizzas!
After lunch we will head back toward St Albans, passing through Park Wood and back onto the edge of the Gorhambury Estate.  As we near St Albans Cathedral we will see sections of the Roman wall bordering the estate, and up to the Great Gateway of the Monastery.  This gateway has existed since roughly AD 948 and served as the entrance gate of the almonry school ( a medieval monastic charity school).  This is now the £30,000 a year Sixth Form St Albans School.
Depending on what time we back at the cathedral grounds there may be time to explore the cathedral itself, although it does close at 5.00 every day.
Our walk will end at the cathedral grounds and we can all make our way back to the station (walking up Holywell Hill to Victoria Street.  Depending on your return train times, feel free to explore the shops and pubs along Victoria Street.
The event booking will close at 17.00 on 12 August, in order to allow the pub ample time to prepare.
Photos provided by
www.unsplash.com
and OutdoorLads members and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Flat walk around St Albans

Title: The Saltings and Moorings of Manhood - The Annual Nudist Beach Walk
Summary:
A manhood or two might well be seen on the isolated naturist beach we'll visit, but Manhood is also the name of the peninsula that stretches south from Chichester to Selsey Bill. It is a strongly horizontal landscape of contrasting blocks of colour. Wide golden wheat fields and broad brown mudflats are backed by big skies, while the regular rows of poplars, the massed masts of yachts and the sporadic spires of churches provide vertical punctuation. Much of the area has been reclaimed from the sea and is drained by the ditches we'll follow, along with the Chichester Canal. The scattered settlements domesticate an expanse so flat that despite walking 17 kilometres, we'll not climb at all.
The sights:
The Manhood Peninsula:
The southernmost part of Sussex and part of the Sussex coastal plain. Bordered to the west by Chichester Harbour and to the east by Pagham Harbour. 'Manhood' comes from the Old English
maene-wudu
meaning "men's wood" or "common wood", i.e. common land, so it isn't nearly as exciting as it sounds.
Chichester Canal:
Opened in 1822, its last load was transported in 1906. Runs 4.5 miles (7.2 km) from Birdham to Chichester. Some of its length is still navigable and the towpath is a popular route.
Hunston:
Not much to write about this village, but pretty Spire Cottage was built using stone reclaimed from Chichester Cathedral tower when it collapsed in 1861.
Sidlesham:
Set among cornfields are half a dozen scattered hamlets, the nicest being Sidlesham Quay beside Pagham Harbour, which looks like a miniature Bosham. We will have lunch here. The historic
Crab & Lobster
pub has a lovely garden.
Pagham Harbour:
A glorious, tranquil area of saltmarsh, mudflats, saltings (for salt extraction) and shallow lagoons. Precious for wildlife as it's an SSSI, a Local Nature Reserve and a Ramsar Site. You could see curlew sandpiper and shelduck on the mudflats and long-tailed tit and willow warbler in the hedges.
Church Norton:
Just the chancel remains from an old church, simple C13, with some fine tombs. The setting is the remarkable thing: surrounded by trees and almost on the shore, the leafy, isolated locale is rare on the Sussex coastal strip. The secluded shingle spit nearby is a nudist beach.
Swimming
This walk
does not
include swimming. If you choose to swim, you do so at your own risk as it is not covered by OutdoorLads's insurance.
The sea water is always clean as there are no sewage outfalls nearby. You're invited to check the quality at Southern Water's
Beachbuoy
site or at the
Surfers Against Sewage
site.
The
route
(please note that this is a linear walk. A bus will take us back to Chichester)
:
We'll leave Chichester by way of the canal and walk along it to Hunston. At Hunston, we'll walk to Hunston Church along Church Lane. We'll then use footpaths to go to South Mundham and use Fisher Lane to Fisher Farm. Further footpaths will take us to Bramber Farm and Honer Farm. We'll then walk around Paghan Harbour to Halsey's Farm and then pretty Sidlesham Quay for lunch. We could also have a drink at the Crab & Lobster pub. Further walking around Pagham Harbour will bring us to the hinge of Pagham Harbour Spit at Church Norton for a break. Footpaths and then a walk along Grange Lane and Rectory Lane will take us to the B2145 and the bus stop.
Getting the bus back:
The No. 51 Stagecoach bus departs for Chichester every 30 minutes (at 33 and 53 minutes past the hour). It takes around 30 minutes to walk from the beach to the bus stop. The fare is £3, payable by any means.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them apart from during very hot weather. There is sea for them to splash in but little shade. Dogs are permitted at Church Norton Beach but there are no poop bins. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

Title: Portsmouth at War, Southsea at Play, the Lads at Sea
Summary:
As this event includes a bespoke
boat tour
, it is £11.50 for Full/Concession Members, and the usual £12.50 day walk cost for PAYG Members (the public would ordinarily pay £13.00 to join a boat tour).
Portsmouth was granted city status in 1926, so on this walk around its most attractive and absorbing areas, we'll see it at a moment of significant civic pride. We'll see the city at war: the historic dockyard with the ships
Warrior
(1860),
Victory
(1759) and the
Mary Rose
(1545), and Southsea Castle from where Henry VIII saw the latter ship sink. We'll see Southsea, Portsmouth's seaside suburb, at play: its esplanade, funfair, pier and rock gardens. We'll see Portsmouth Harbour from the water too, as I have chartered a boat to take us on an exclusive hour-long tour. We'll see ferries and frigates and aircraft carriers and cruise ships, and we'll see why Portsmouth is home to two thirds of our naval fleet.
What you could do in Portsmouth and Southsea after the walk:
Sunbathe on a beach nearby: Head back to the Hot Walls in Old Portsmouth. I will have shown you where this is. And Portsmouth also has a nudist beach.
Explore the ships in the Historic Dockyard: Click
here
. 12-month tickets are £51, although there is a cheaper afternoon ticket if arriving after 14.00, I believe.
Shop in Gunwharf Quays: There's a range of outlet shopping to be had with many big brands.
Ascend the Spinnaker Tower in Gunwharf Quays: Click
here
. Tickets are £15.50 (or £10.50 if bought online).
Go the Blue Reef Aquarium: Click
here
. Day tickets bought online are £15.95
The sights and
route
(please see this at the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Starting at Portsmouth Harbour Railway Station, we'll walk northwest along The Hard, to enter, having had our bags checked, the...
1. Historic Dockyard:
Established from the late C17, greatly expanded in the Georgian and Victorian periods. A large and coherent complex of naval buildings and basins, of outstanding historical importance, though architecturally varied. The Historic Dockyard is the publicly-accessible part of HM Naval Base Portsmouth. Visitor attractions include the ships
Warrior
,
Victory
and the
Mary Rose
. The Rope Houses are among the earliest cast iron-framed buildings in Britain, landmarks in industrial design. The Historic Dockyard is free to enter and walk round, although the ships in it aren't.
And continuing deeper into the dockyard, we'll see...
2. HMS Warrior:
1860, by Isaac Watts and Thomas Lloyd. Iron-hulled, armoured warship. A landmark in naval engineering. Built in response to the launching of the steam-powered ship
Napoleon
by France in 1850,
Warrior
's arrival was hastened by the launch of France's
Gloire
in 1859. It began an arms race between France and Britain that lasted for a decade.
Warrior
never saw action and became obsolete in 1873 when Britain launched the mastless HMS
Devastation
. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
Then, further on...
3. HMS M.33:
One of the last surviving ships from the Great War. Built in 1915 under the direct orders of Winston Churchill, then First Lord of the Admiralty, HMS
M.33
is the only British ship still standing from the Gallipoli Campaign (1915-1916).
And close by...
4. HMS Victory:
1759, by Sir Thomas Slade. Three-decker ship of the line and Nelson’s flagship at Trafalgar. A masterpiece of C18 naval architecture. Now at risk of collapsing under its own weight and mostly enclosed in scaffolding, its restoration will be complete in 2030. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to board it.
And finally in the Dockyard...
5. The Mary Rose Museum:
2013, by Wilkinson Eyre Architects. A dark, angular envelope enclosing the conserved hull of Henry VIII’s carrack from 1545. A dramatic and controlled space enclosing its central exhibit: most of the starboard side and keel of the ship, lifted from the bed of The Solent in 1982. The artefacts recovered are a Tudor time-capsule. Unless you stay after the walk finishes, we won't be paying to enter the museum.
We'll then leave the Historic Dockyard and walk along to Hard to enter...
6. Gunwharf Quays:
A redevelopment of 1990s–2001 on the former HMS
Vernon
site, a naval scrapyard and ordnance supply base. A mixed commercial ensemble of limited architectural distinction, but red brick was used in deference to conservation interests. Dominated by the Spinnaker Tower (2005, 170 m), the sweeping, sail-like form of which provides a crude but beloved landmark on the harbour skyline. Survivals from before redevelopment include the Vulcan Building (No. 1 Storehouse), 1814–15, a long brick naval store by the Admiralty, with austere but refined industrial character, now adapted to commercial use. A crane for lifting shells, torpedoes and ships' figureheads evoke sense of place. The redevelopment is highly successful commercially and socially, opening up a prime piece of waterfront.
We'll then have our boat trip at 12.15 around...
7. Portsmouth Harbour:
A large natural harbour, its size and double high tide (owing to the narrowness of the entrance) most advantageous for shipping. Also protected by the Isle of Wight across The Solent. An important RAMSAR wetland site but developed all round the edge. The commentary on board the charter boat will indicate the naval base, the cruise ship terminal, the tropical fruit import quay and the submarine museum at Gosport. No cruise ships will be in on the date, but ferries will be seen in abundance. The Navy doesn't publish lists of ships present, but Portsmouth is home to the aircraft carriers
Queen Elizabeth
and
Prince of Wales
and the Type-45 Destroyers
Daring, Dauntless, Diamond, Dragon, Defender
and
Duncan
.
Disembarking the boat, we'll follow the Millennium Walkway round to Gunwharf Road and White Hart Road and we'll reach Feltham Row, a walkway beside...
8. The Camber:
Taking its name from the River Camber (now built over), the site of the medieval and Tudor dockyard. Now the home of the city's modest fishing fleet.
By going along northwest along Broad Street then round Bath Square and Tower Street we can see see some of...
9. Old Portsmouth:
The area covered by the original medieval town of Portsmouth. Situated in the south west corner of Portsea Island. The roads still largely follow their original layout. Plenty of distinctive maritime character but mostly rebuilt after wartime devastation. A very pleasant part of the city.
In particular, we'll enjoy...
10. Portsmouth Point/Spice Island:
Historic waterfront quarter associated with victualling and sailors’ taverns. Much rebuilt, post-war, but retains a degree of maritime character. The inspiration for Thomas Rowlandson's rollicking engraving Portsmouth Point and hence Sir William Walton's overture of the same name. Great views over the Harbour to Gosport here.
Ascending onto the defensive ramparts at the Hot Walls we'll pass through the...
11. Round Tower:
1418, rebuilt later C15 in stone. Cylindrical harbour defence, visually more striking than the Square Tower, its curved form well suited to its exposed position. A feature familiar from news broadcasts where the city gathers to watch naval vessels departing and arriving.
And then through the...
12. Square Tower:
1494, later modified. A solid, rectilinear artillery tower guarding the harbour entrance. Plain but powerful, with thick masonry and minimal ornament.
This is all by the Millennium Trail and promenade which we will descend to the High Street to see...
13. The Dolphin:
A fine C18 pub in which Nelson stayed, scratching his name with a diamond ring on a window pane.
And opposite...
14. Portsmouth Cathedral:
Parish church of St Thomas dating from 1180, chancel dating to C13. Largely rebuilt after Civil War devastation in 1642, including choir and tower rebuilt and cupola added in 1660s after the Restoration. Made cathedral 1927. Nave extended 1930s by Charles Nicholson in a simplified Norman-Byzantine idiom. The rebuilding interrupted by war, it was only finished in 1991. Modest in scale; an interesting and pleasing blend of post-medieval survival and interwar ecclesiastical design.
Then we'll head southeast down Pembroke Road, Duisburg Way and Clarence Parade to enter the town of Southsea and...
15. Southsea Common:
Open ground established from the C18 as part of a defensive glacis, later landscaped as a public amenity. Expansive and informal, valued more for setting and views across the Solent than for designed features. The large English elm trees are a notable asset, protected from Dutch elm disease by Portsmouth's isolation.
We'll follow a path (Serpentine Road) to emerge at Clarence Esplanade where we'll turn left to see...
16. Landing Craft LCT 7074:
1944. The last surviving Landing Craft Tank from D-Day operations, now restored and linked with the D-Day Museum. Purely functional naval engineering, its interest entirely historical.
At the D-Day Museum we'll head south to...
17. Southsea Castle.
1544, for Henry VIII, attributed to Richard Lee. A low, angular artillery fort of early modern type, later altered but demonstrating advances in gunnery made in C16. Vantage point from which Henry saw his flagship Mary Rose capsize in 1545. Rugged and compact, retaining much of its original defensive character. Stumpy lighthouse with foghorn. Free to enter, which we will. We can also have tea or coffee here.
We'll then proceed northwest along the promenade to see the new...
18. Sea defences:
A £185 million coastal defence scheme is rebuilding 4.5 km of seafront to reduce flood risk to over 10,000 properties. Works include widening the beach with imported shingle, raising promenades, and installing new barriers. Ongoing phases (2024–2027) involve closures and upgrades, improving resilience against storms and rising sea levels. The stretch at Clarence Esplanade we'll walk along was only opened in April.
And further along Clarence Esplanade we'll see...
19. World War I and World War II Memorials.
Naval Memorial, 1924, by Sir Robert Lorrimer. Impressive and abstract, with naval motifs sometimes likened to a columna rostrata (with ships' prows protruding at the top). Identical to memorials at Chatham and Plymouth. Flanking additions after 1945 by Sir Edward Maufe, carefully handled to preserve the unity of the ensemble. The 2-metre statues of service personnel in various garbs are imposing.
Further still along Clarence Esplanade, we'll reach...
20. Southsea Hoverport:
The journey to Ryde takes 10 minutes and is (the operators claim) the only year-round commercial hovercraft service still operating in the world. Hovertravel operates a pair of craft capable of 40 knots.
We'll then go past Clarence Pier along...
21. Long Curtain and Battery:
C17 sea defences, an earthwork and masonry rampart. Altered, but legible as part of the continuous line of coastal fortification stretching around Old Portsmouth.
Well then descend the fortification to the...
22. Royal Garrison Church:
Domus Dei, founded 1212 as hospice and chapel. Later adapted for military use. The marriage of Charles II of England to Catherine of Braganza took place here in 1662. Nave left roofless after 1941 bombing, chancel restored. A moving ruin, its open shell of a nave contrasting with the intact chancel.
Walking up Penny Road and then turning left along Pembroke Road, we'll the turn right up the High Street past...
23. Buckingham House (Ye Spotted Dogge):
Late C15 or early C16. Timber-framed, with later alterations; one of the oldest survivals in Portsmouth, refronted C18. Traditionally associated with John Felton, who killed George Villiers, 1st Duke of Buckingham (1628), favourite of James I of England.
And further up...
24. Portsmouth Grammar School:
Founded in 1732, from when the present restrained but suave frontispiece dates. Selective private school. Alumni are known as Old Portsmuthians and include Roger Black, Mel Stride, Murray Gold and Ant Middleton. And...
At Cambridge Junction we'll turn left and walk along St George's Road past...
25. Landport Gate: 1760–63:
Modest Portland stone triumphal arch forming part of the fortified enceinte (enclosure of naval buildings). A restrained classical composition with rusticated base and central arch, its original defensive context lost.
We'll then walk north along Ordnance Row back to The Hard and the railway station. Then you are welcome to do as you wish. There are some suggestions below.
Dogs:
This walk is partly suitable for dogs. There are stretches where they can walk extensively on and off the lead, but they are not permitted on the beaches we'll pass, nor are they permitted in the naval base. Hot weather could make the walk exhausting for them.
(All images were taken by the leader.)
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 9 km (5.5 miles); relief is flat; terrain is gravel, tarmac and concrete.

Title: Go Ape 🦧
Summary:
Go Ape
Climb, cross and zip through the treetops of Crawley — high ropes thrills in a stunning woodland setting.
Push your limits on their most popular high ropes course, packed with tree-to-tree crossings, Tarzan Swings and epic zip lines.
Experience their boldest adventure in the heart of Tilgate Park. This self belay course will have you navigating your way across wobbly bridges and soaring through the canopy on five exhilarating zip lines up to 146m long. Take on five sections with adrenaline-fueled challenges and high-flying thrills, including suspended bridges, two of our legendary Tarzan Swings and the tough ‘stirrups’ crossing.
Booking
Visit the link below and book the
tree top challenge
for 2pm on Saturday 15th August price is
£40.95 at the Crawley Tilgate Park site.
Secure your place now
It is important that you do this straight away to ensure you secure your space on the event.
The maximum weight to participate is 130kg / 20.5 stone
This is a perfect event for members old and new to catch up with each other, find out about upcoming events and let their hair down.

Title: Round Dunsop Bridge on a Thursday
Summary:
Join Stephen on an
11 mile
walk in and around
Dunsop Bridge
in
Lancashire
in the heart of the Forest of Bowland and in the centre of Great Britain.
We will start by following the River Hodder for a while then climb up to onto the moors overlooking the Hodder Valley.
We will venture through many farms and walk along the tops with views across to the Bowland Fells then returning back to Dunsop Bridge.
When we arrive back in the village -let's find the Centre of Great Britain marker!
There is no pub in the village  - but we can take a short drive to the wonderful Inn at Whitewell for some post walk drinks - here is a link -
http://www.innatwhitewell.com/
Image / photo credits:  All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Don’t worry if the event is full.
Do sign up and join the waiting list.
Often those on the main list cancel nearer the time.
There is a very high chance you will still get a place
Difficulty & Distance: There is slow ascent up onto the fells

Title: An Evening with the Stars in Nottinghamshire
Summary:
We have secured a booking for a group visit to
Sherwood Observatory
.
Since its recent re-opening, the Observatory has been booked solid with group tours so we are pleased to offer this opportunity in Autumn 2026.
The visit promises a 90 minute adventure you'll never forget including a chance to ask anything you want to know about space and beyond. Additionally there will be:
a 20+ minute planetarium show and presentation by the expert team
a captivating talk in the lecture room that will spark your imagination
a thrilling opportunity to gaze at the stars and planets or our closest star, the Sun, depending on the season, (weather dependent)
a tour of the radio astronomy facilities
a visit of exhibition in the Science Centre to learn more about human space flight and the history of the Reservoir
Photos: Sherwood Observatory and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: Sit and watch the stars go by. The site is broadly accessible but visiting the main telescope involves climbing some steps

Title: Isle of Portland Climbing and Walking Weekend (3 nights)
Summary:
Join us for an exciting
three-night
weekend of climbing and walking on the tiny island of Portland, Dorset, well-known for its rugged beauty, long coastline, and incredible climbing. Our accommodation is located across the street from the beach, so a lovely setting in which to enjoy the summer!
David (
david209)
will lead the outdoor climbing events on Saturday and Sunday. Spaces are limited to 14 climbers on each day, including two leaders. It is a great opportunity for newer members and novice climbers to meet and learn from more experienced OutdoorLads.
Please sign-up to your preferred
climbing
events separately (below):
Saturday climbing
Sunday climbing
Peter (
MaccMan
) will lead the walking events on Saturday and Sunday.
Please sign up to your preferred
walking
events separately (below):
Saturday walk
- Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Sunday walk
- Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
The bunk rooms are simple, each with a small sink and private shower. Bedding and towels are provided. There is a spacious communal lounge and kitchen, as well as toilets, all on the first floor.
Please note
, this is a three-night hostel event from Friday evening to Monday morning to allow two full days of activities over the weekend.
Waitlist:
If an event is fully booked, please
add yourself to the waitlist.
Places often become available closer to the date. If you decide not to attend,
cancel your attendance
as soon as possible to allow someone else to take your place.
Regrettably, the hostel does not have disabled access due to very narrow hallways and all communal space being on the first floor with no lift access.
Dogs:
Sorry, dogs are not permitted at this accommodation.
Want to Get Out More?

http://outdoorlads.events/SouthWestNotifications

Courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
, The Bunker and Derventio, and used with permission and thanks.

Title: A Day of Climbing in Portland - Saturday (Beginners Welcome)
Summary:
Join
David
and
Neil
on the stunning island of Portland for some adrenaline-pumping sport climbing! This is a fantastic opportunity to get out on the famous limestone cliffs overlooking the English Channel. Whether you're a seasoned sport climber or have been leading indoors and want to venture on to some real rock, this event is perfect for you.
For those who haven't done sport climbing before, there will be a quick tutorial session at the start of the event, you just need to be competent at tying in, top rope belaying and have a pair of climbing shoes. If you've done three or more indoor climbing events in recent history, that's about all the experience you need. If you are unsure about the difficulty of the event, or whether you have necessary skills & experience, please message the event leader.
On this day, we will explore some of the best climbing areas that Portland has to offer. There will be a range of grades available to cater to all levels of experience, so if you haven't climbed outdoors before, just let the organiser know when you sign up so we can make sure we have a good mix of experiences.
The event is part of an OutdoorLads hostel weekend, with some members taking part in a walking event. After the day's climbing, we will return to the Bunker Hostel for a well-deserved rest and enjoy a friendly and sociable evening. Make sure that if you'd like to stay in the hostel, you
book the hostel event separately here.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
Photos:
Taken by OutdoorLads members and used with full permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Climbing requires a reasonable level of fitness and agility

Title: Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk near Dorchester that includes Maiden Castle, a length of the South Dorset Ridgeway and the Hardy Monument!
The route is 11.5 miles / 18.5 kilometres, with 430 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
Maiden Castle
is the largest and most impressive Iron Age hillfort in Britain, covering an area bigger than 50 football pitches.  Work on the complex defensive site began about 2,400 years ago and continued for 300 years.  When it was first built, the gleaming white chalk ramparts would have towered over the surrounding landscape.  At the hillfort’s peak of development, hundreds of people lived there in a settlement of thatched buildings. Many had central hearths for cooking, warmth and light.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route until a lower level path was created.  It now provides an alternative, higher level, route with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his novels.
The highpoint of today’s walk is the impressive
Hardy Monument
, built in 1844 in memory of Vice-Admiral Sir
Thomas Masterman Hardy
, Captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar.  Hardy is remembered because, amongst other things, it was in his arms that Nelson died, saying the immortal words ‘Kiss me, Hardy’.
The Hardy Monument is a stone tower, built on the summit of Black Down and designed to look like a spyglass, which Admiral Hardy would have used on board ship.  It is 22 metres / 72 feet high and may be open for us to climb its 120 steps when we pass by.  On a clear day you can see Devon and the the Isle of Wight, both of which are around 40 miles / 65 kilometres away, and to the north Pen Hill in the Mendip Hills, which is a similar distance away.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk along part of Chesil Beach, returning along part of the South Dorset Ridgeway!
The route is 9 miles / 15 kilometres, with 300 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
One of the most significant features along Dorset’s 95-mile
Jurassic Coast
is
Chesil Beach
.  The name Chesil is derived from the Old English
ceosel
or
cisel
, meaning gravel or shingle.  Over 18 miles / 29 kilometres long and up to 220 yards / 200 metres wide, the beach is estimated to be 100 million tonnes of pebbles.  It rises to 16 yards / 15 metres above sea level.  Thought to date back some 6,000 years, the beach is the result of onshore currents and rising sea levels.  It is moving inland at a rate of 6 yards / 5 metres per century.
Interestingly, the pebbles are naturally graded in size from fist-sized near Portland (at the eastern end of the beach) to pea-sized at Bridport (at the western end of the beach). The pebbles are mainly a mix of flint and chert.  The beach shelters the
Fleet
, which is Britain’s largest tidal lagoon.  The water is up to 3 metres deep. The Fleet is home to many wading birds and the
Abbotsbury Swannery
.  This is a managed colony of nesting swans, thought to have been established by monks at the former monastery of Abbotsbury.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route (the route around Portland was only created in 2003).  It now provides an alternative route to walking over the pebbles of Chesil Beach, with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his stories.
The walk starts and ends in the village of
Abbotsbury
, which is known for its picturesque historic buildings and thatched cottages.  It was the site of a monastery, which fell victim to King Henry VIII’s dissolution of monastic establishments in the mid-16th century.  There are some remains of the abbey, including a large tithe barn (built circa 1400) and St Catherine’s Chapel, a 14th century pilgrimage chapel that stands on a hill between the village and the coast.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Brighton: Break In or Break Out? You Decide!
Summary:
Think you’re clever enough to crack a vault? Fast enough to break out of jail? Put your skills to the test in this two-part escape room challenge—where one wrong move could land you behind bars… and only the smartest teams make it.
You will only play one of the games if you have played one of them before please let the leader know or equally if you have a preference we will try and accommodate that.
The cost is £18 per player payable on the day.
Escape the cell
You’ve been arrested, and now it’s time to use your grey matter and work as a team to escape your cell whilst handcuffed to your team mates.
Rob a Vault
Hidden inside Presuming Ed’s Bar & Coffee House is one of Brighton’s oldest bank vaults. Be hired to rob an old HSBC bank vault.
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Want to Get Out More?
To be informed of all our South Coast events* events please click on this Telegram link
https://t.me/joinchat/AAAAAFTkXZnLlg4gD7B5nw
* Kent, East Sussex, West Sussex, Hampshire, Isle of Wight, Surrey

Photos taken by Outdoor Lads member Cassidyi with permission for Outdoor Lads use.

Title: Slovenia Adventure
Summary:
Visit the beautiful country of Slovenia for a week of trekking and activities in the stunning Julian Alps with OutdoorLads.
An action-packed week. A summit climb. White-water rafting, Canyoning and stunning alpine landscapes!
Our main objective will be to climb Mt Triglav, Slovenia's highest mountain and to trek in the surrounding Triglavski National Park. Triglav is a low altitude peak (2864m) that involves scrambling to reach the summit. The ridge is equipped with an iron walkway called a Via Ferrata to help us reach the top securely and safely. A good head for heights and good walking fitness is required. During this four day trek we will be staying in mountain huts, similar to hostels and eating local cuisine.
After the four day trek in the stunning Julian Alps we will travel to Bovec to raft on the famous emerald Soca river. This river runs through Slovenia and Italy and eventually drains into the Adriatic Sea near Trieste.
Our final night will be spent in a hostel in Ljubljana where there will be the opportunity to do some sightseeing in the capital of this friendly little country.
This price for this action-packed week is
only £1350
which includes 7-nights accommodation in hostels and mountain huts.
What is included?
All Accomodation
UIMLA International Mountain Leader
All Breakfasts
Dinner and lunch when trekking
Canyoning on the first day (this will be a beginners canyon but you can opt out if needed)
Via Feratta kit, harness and helmet and a Slovenian Mountain Guide for Triglav
White-Water rafting in Bovec (this is suitable for beginners it is also possible to opt out)
What is not included?
Flights to and from Ljubljana airport
Food and drinks in Ljubljana
Lunch in Bled
Insurance - you will need travel insurance to include the activities
More information on this trip is available from the
Pride Expeditions website
ITINERARY
Day 1  Arrival, 29th August 2026
Transfer from Ljubljana Airport directly to Bled (45 mins). Dinner in a local restaurant with traditional Slovenian food or Pizza. Accommodation at a hostel in Bled.
Day 2 Half-day Canyoning, 30th August
We will be canyoning in Bled & Bohinj area in Jereka gorge (easy) or Grmecica gorge (moderate level) near Bled. No experience required all equipment provided. Upon return there will be time for lunch and a relaxing afternoon in Bled. Tonight we will have dinner in a traditional restaurant in Bled.
Day 3 Hiking Bled to Kredarica Hut (2500m), 31st August
After breakfast we will travel by bus to the Pokljuka Plateau. We hike up to Kredarica Mt Hut. This is a 6hr steady climb we will stop for a packed lunch along the route.  Dinner and accomodation in Kredarica hut.
Day 4 Hiking Kredarica Hut to Triglav Lakes Hut (1685m), 1st September
After breakfast we will via ferrata climb to the top of Mt Triglav and then descend to lunch. After some rest and photos we will walk to Triglav Lakes Hut. Have dinner at the hut and overnight. 8hrs walking.
Day 5 Hiking Triglav Lakes Hut to Krn Lakes Hut ,2nd September
We will walk from Triglav Lakes to Komna mountain hut. This is where we will stop for lunch before continuing through the Bogatin area and onwards to Vratca saddle. After crossing the saddle we descend to Krn lakes hut. This is where we will have dinner and spend the night.  6 - 7 hrs walking.
Day 6 Hiking and rafting Krn Lakes hut to Lepena valley and onwards to Bovec, 3rd September
We walk down from Krn Lakes into the stunning Lepena Valley. After visiting a few historical sites from WW1 we will be picked up and transferred to Bovec for white water rafting on the emerald Soca River. In the evening we eat and have a few drinks and our evening meal in Bovec.
Day 7  Transfer day Bovec - Ljubljana 4th September
We will be transferred from Bovec to Ljubljana (4hrs) where we will be staying in a boutique hostel in the Metelkova district of Ljubljana. You will have some time to visit the imposing castles and pretty bridges of downtown Ljubljana before our final meal in Slovenia. The hostel will be in walking distance of the city centre.
Day 8  Ljubljana to Home 5th September
We will be transferred to the airport to connect with your flight back to the UK.
GETTING THERE
You are responsible for organising your own flight to Ljubljana. Easy Jet run a daily flight from London Gatwick and from Edinburgh, there are also direct British Airways flights from London Heathrow. Or if you want to fly from Manchester or Bristol you will need to fly with Lufthansa and change in Frankfurt
THE ACCOMODATION
Whilst in Bled, Bovec and Ljubljana we will be in hostels in a mixture of triple and quad rooms. In the mountain huts we tend to be in one or two dormitory rooms depending on availability. Single rooms are not possible on this trip. All the hostels we use are locally owned and friendly and welcoming. They will be within walking distance of the local sights.
HOW TO BOOK
Bookings and Deposits: The full price is £1350.
To book your place on this trip (or secure a place on the waiting list) please pay a £250 deposit direct to Pride Expeditions. This will secure your place on the trip.
PRIDE EXPEDITIONS Ltd
Starling Bank
Sort Code:60-83-71
Account Number 19166775
Please message the Event Leader with your full name and email address in order to receive further documents.
On receipt of your deposit a confirmation email will be sent. Attached will be a comprehensive medical form for you to fill in as well as booking terms and conditions. Once six people have paid a deposit the trip will be guaranteed and further payments can be made in instalments. If you cancel after the trip has been confirmed and bookings have been made in Slovenia you will lose your deposit. All monies must be paid by the 2nd of July 2026. Deposits are fully refundable until the trip is guaranteed and bookings are made at which point the terms and conditions will come into force.
Please be aware that it is the booking of the deposit that secures your place on the trip or waiting list, rather than booking onto the trip via the OutdoorLads site.
Image Credits:
All images copyright and supplied by Nick Read.
Difficulty & Distance: 8-action packed days including a 4-day trek

Title: BIG20: THE Celebration Event - Hostel
Summary:
Where did the 20 years go!? It's been an epic tens of thousands of miles walked, climbed, skied, kayaked, cycled; thousands of friendships made; and an immeasurable amount of laughter and incredible memories.
We hope you can join us in the Peak District to celebrate this epic milestone!
There are three accommodation types for this event, so please ensure you book on the correct one:
Hostel
(this event page)
Pods
Camping
All accommodation types include the catering and entertainment in the main building.
The hostel has a range of modern, all-ensuite rooms with bunk beds. They are spacious and well appointed, this is a lovely hostel!
The weekend will include a formal AGM which all Full/Concession Members are very much encouraged to attend to help us formally celebrate our amazing volunteers and members. (If you want to attend just the AGM and not stay at the hostel or for the party, that will be possible and details will be made available in due course, along with details of the AGM live stream.)
You can also expect amazing walks and other events offered by our volunteers, on-site activities (is that a zip wire on top of the building!?), spectacular food, a fully-stocked bar, a late night disco with DJ Dan, and we're going to party like its 2016 with a Ceilidh with Triple Scotch (yes, they want to come back!)!
Whether you have been a member since 2006 when we formed or only just joined us today - all are welcome and all have played a part creating, shaping and continuing this wonderful organisation which has changed lives, brought lifelong friends together (and even husbands!), and given everyone the chance to try something new with our wide range of activities for all.
You don't want to miss out on this epic weekend, so get booking!
Part Payment
If you'd like to pay in instalments, please contact us on
support@outdoorlads.com
and we can arrange that. We'll discuss with you how much you'd like to pay each month and a time frame over which you'd like to pay. You will need to have paid in full by the 31 August.
OutdoorLads Foundation
If this event is not financially viable to you, check out the
OutdoorLads Foundation
, and hopefully we can get you celebrating with us!
Dogs:
Unfortunately, dogs are not permitted at this venue, sorry.
Campervans/Caravans/Motorhomes:
This event is not suitable for campervans, motorhomes or caravans.
Photos provided by the venue and by OutdoorLads Members with permission for use.
Difficulty & Distance: Hostel celebration event with other ancillary events

Title: Bruges Blast: A North Sea Adventure
Summary:
OutdoorLads are sailing across the North Sea to Belgium!
We're delighted to offer this event following the success of OutdoorLads x CatZero's "Dutch Dash" in 2025!
You do not need any past sailing experience for this. This is an incredible opportunity to spend five days aboard CatZero, as we take on this 400-mile round-trip across the North Sea from the Humber estuary to Zeebrugge and back (a "Bruges Blast")!
CatZero is a 72-foot Challenge Round the World Racing Yacht. Category Zero is the rating of the vessel, meaning she can sail anywhere in the world in all conditions. CatZero is also the name of the charity that own and operate the vessel.
On this voyage, you will be crewing the boat under the instruction and direction of the fantastic CatZero team. Absolute beginner with no sailing experience, or total sailing pro? It doesn't matter if you've never set foot on a boat before or if you're a qualified sailor, these trips are for everyone. You'll learn what you need to know to sail the ship.
This is not a leisure holiday! Voyages aboard CatZero are interactive, fun and rewarding. You'll be living in very close proximity with the rest of team, and working together throughout the five days. Whilst sailing (approximately 24 hours each way) we will split into two watches, alternating every four hours to rest/work, including through the night. You'll take your role in helping run the boat, from cooking, cleaning, scrubbing the decks and helping sail throughout your trip. Team work and camaraderie will be in abundance, and we're sure you'll have an absolutely fantastic time on board. You'll be exhausted by the end of the week, but you will have had the absolute time of your lives, learn a lot about sailing, yourself, and each other - this is the kind of trip that creates special memories.
Past sailing experience is not necessary - there will be an experienced crew on board who will show you everything you need to do. If you've never sailed before but think you'd enjoy it, and are prepared to get stuck in and give it your all for a five days, then this is a perfect event for you! Included as part of this trip, you can even complete the
RYA Competent Crew qualification
!
On arrival into Zeebrugge on the Saturday morning, we'll put the sails away and then be able to have a shower, refresh ourselves, and have a restful explore of the city, or just plonk ourselves somewhere for some refreshments in the July sunshine. Depending on timing, passport control, and transport available, we may be able to venture into the centre of Bruges. You are also free to do your own thing.
If you have any questions before you book please feel free to contact the event Leader.
Highlight reel from our 2025 sail to The Netherlands >
Here's an article
about one of CatZero's recent Dutch Dashes, where you can also learn about the remarkable charity, bringing about positive change in peoples' lives.
Join us for a tour of CatZero on Sunday 1st March 2026, 10:30am >
(to coincide with the
Hull social
the night before)
Can't make the tour, but want another opportunity to check out CatZero?
Let us know
and we'll see what we can do!
Payment
We're pleased to be able to bring this event to you £39 cheaper than last year!
If you would prefer to pay in monthly instalments, please
contact the office team
(or
raise a support ticket
) and we can discuss the options available to you, depending on the time available between booking and the event taking place. There will be additional terms and conditions to agree to before you commit. You won't pay anything extra to pay in instalments. This is how the event leader has chosen to pay - so it's no hardship.
Examples of instalment options, but we're happy to discuss options with you:
Book during January: 6 x £125
Book during February: 5 x £150
Book during March: £195 + (3 x £185)
Book during April: 3 x £250
All money owed must be received by the end of June 2026.
OutdoorLads Foundation
'Foundation' places are available on this event, with up to a £200 grant available towards the event cost for those who may find this difficult to access financially.
Find out more about OutdoorLads Foundation and apply
. You will still be able to pay the remaining amount in instalments.
Some Ts&Cs
CatZero's and OutdoorLads' Liability
CatZero or OutdoorLads will take no responsibility for loss or damage of personal belongings on this event, and will accept no liability.
CatZero or OutdoorLads have no liability to the participant in the event the participant fails to board the yacht prior to sailing, unless caused by the negligence of CatZero or its representatives.
Health
You must be mentally and physically fit for the sail. All participants must be in general good health.
You will be required to complete a medical questionnaire prior to sailing, and you must make CatZero aware of any injuries, illnesses and/or medications you are taking. You must notify CatZero of any changes between comlpetion of the medical questionnaire and the sailing.
CatZero can refuse to accept you if they believe that the safety and/or smooth running of the sail would be at risk due to any injuries or illness a participant has, or due to any other medical reason.
Safety
The safety of all participants is of paramount importance to CatZero. Sailing can be dangerous and hazardous, and all participants must accept those risks and always have regard to the safety of themselves and third parties.
Whilst on the yacht, all participants must comply at all times with the authority, decisions, and instructions of the Skipper. All participants must comply with with all safety guidelines and instructions given by the Skipper and other CatZero representatives.
All participants must not engage in any activity, whether shore based or on board the yacht, which is likely to compromise their safety or the safety of any other person.
Failure to comply may result in the Skipper ask the individual to vacate the yacht.
The Skipper or CatZero will have the absolute authority, without providing any prior notice, ask an individual or individuals to vacate the yacht if the safety or smooth running of the sail is deemed to be at risk.
In the event that an individual is asked to vacate the yacht, CatZero will not be liable for costs incurred.
Insurance
CatZero or OutdoorLads do not provide any insurance covering the participant.
The participant is responsible for making their own arrangements for adequate insurance to cover the cost of personal accident, personal liability, medical expenses, and emergency repatriation during the sail, including travel to/from the yacht.
The participant agrees to indemnify CatZero in respect of any costs or expenses claimed against CatZero or incurred by CatZero which would ordinarily be covered by adequate insurance taken out by the participant.
Behaviour
Throughout the period of the sail, both when ashore and on board the yacht, all participants shall:
behave in a moderate and reasonable manner, taking into account of the people around them
do nothing which may render the yacht liable to arrest or impounding
not take or consume personal stocks of alcohol on board the yacht
not behave in a manner that is or is likely to be disruptive, abusive, antisocial, dangerous, or likely to cause upset or distress to any third party or damage to property
Not use, carry, or store any illegal drugs, substances or any other illegal items
Not engage in any activity which is likely to compromise the reputation of CatZero
If in the Skipper's reasonable opinion, any participant is in breach of the above, in any way, they shall have the absolute authority, without providing prior notice, to ask them to vacate the yacht.

Provided by OutdoorLads Members on our 2025 sail to The Netherlands
